href="/static/fonts/inter-bold-optimized.woff2" type="font/woff2" crossorigin="anonymous"/><style>
	/* latin */
	@font-face {
		font-family: 'Inter';
		font-style: normal;
		font-weight: 400;
		font-display: swap;
		src: local('Inter Regular'), local('Inter-Regular'),
			url('/static/fonts/inter-regular-optimized.woff2') format('woff2'), /* Chrome 26+, Opera 23+, Firefox 39+ */
			url('/static/fonts/inter-regular-optimized.woff') format('woff'); /* Chrome 6+, Firefox 3.6+, IE 9+, Safari 5.1+ */
	}
	/* latin */
	@font-face {
		font-family: 'Inter';
		font-style: normal;
		font-weight: bold;
		font-display: swap;
		src: local('Inter Heavy'), local('Inter-Heavy'),
			url('/static/fonts/inter-bold-optimized.woff2') format('woff2'), /* Chrome 26+, Opera 23+, Firefox 39+ */
			url('/static/fonts/inter-bold-optimized.woff') format('woff'); /* Chrome 6+, Firefox 3.6+, IE 9+, Safari 5.1+ */
	}
	* {
		margin: 0;
		padding: 0;
		box-sizing: border-box;
		-webkit-box-sizing: border-box;
	}
	html, body {
		height: 100%;
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
		color: #1C2838;
		font-size: 16px;
	}
	html {
		height: 100%;
		width: 100%;
		-webkit-font-smoothing: subpixel-antialiased;
	}

	:root {
		--black: #1a1a1a;
		--white: #ffffff;

		--title-font: Inter, -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, Segoe UI, Roboto, Oxygen-Sans, Ubuntu, Cantarell, Helvetica Neue, sans-serif;
		--copy-font: Inter, -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, Segoe UI, Roboto, Oxygen-Sans, Ubuntu, Cantarell, Helvetica Neue, sans-serif;
		--font-base: 16px;
		--font-base-height: 25px;

		--h1-hero-size: 48px;
		--h1-size: 40px;
		--h2-size: 32px;
		--h3-size: 24px;
		--h4-size: 16px;

		—-breakpoint: 992px;
		—-breakpoint-mobile: 420px;
		—-breakpoint-desktop: 1200px;

		--success: #49a147;
		--warning: #ffad0d;
		--error: #e35141;

		--box-shadow: 0px 5px 20px -5px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.3);
		--box-shadow-100: 0px 1px 3px 1px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.15),
		0px 1px 2px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.3);

		--gray-100: #f8f8f8;
		--gray-200: #eeeeee;
		--gray-300: #cccccc;
		--gray-400: #6a6a6a;
		--gray-500: #1a1a1a;

		--primary: #1d2758;
		--primary-100: #e4e5eb;
		--primary-200: #8e93ac;
		--primary-300: #1d2758;
		--primary-400: #151d47;


		--secondary: #eff6f7;
		--secondary-100: #E5EBF5;
		--secondary-200: #92ACD3;
		--secondary-300: #2459A9;
		--secondary-400: #163E8F;

		--tertiary: #2459a9;
		--tertiary-100: #FAFCFD;
		--tertiary-200: #F4F9F9;
		--tertiary-300: #EFF6F7;
		--tertiary-400: #E4EFF1;

		--interactive: #c77124;
		--interactive-100: #f8eee5;
		--interactive-200: #e3b892;
		--interactive-300: #c77124;
		--interactive-400: #ba5e1b;

		--accent-200: #F4F9F9;

		--focus: #005FCC;

		--gradient-100: linear-gradient(183.11deg, rgba(248, 238, 229, 0.4) 2.57%, rgba(229, 235, 245, 0.4) 97.42%);
		--gradient-200: linear-gradient(90deg, #EFF6F7 0.02%, #F8EEE5 99.96%);
	}

	h1 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 36px;
		line-height: 44px;
	}
	h2 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 30px;
		line-height: 40px;
		margin: 30px 0 24px;
	}
	h3 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 24px;
		line-height: 32px;
		margin: 30px 0 24px;
	}
	h4 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 20px;
		line-height: 32px;
	}

	p, span, a {
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
	}
	p {
		font-size: 16px;
		line-height: 25px;
		margin: 26px 0;
	}
	a {
		color: var(--interactive);
	}


	/* Same styles as p but can be used on <div>s */
	.bold{
		font-weight: bold;
	}
	.text-4xl {
		font-size: 36px;
		line-height: 44px;
	}
	.text-3xl {
		font-size: 30px;
		line-height: 40px;
	}
	.text-2xl {
		font-size: 24px;
		line-height: 32px;
	}
	.text-xl {
		font-size: 20px;
		line-height: 32px;
	}
	.text-lg {
		font-size: 18px;
		line-height: 28px;
	}
	.text-base {
		font-size: 16px;
		line-height: 24px;
	}
	.text-sm {
		font-size: 14px;
		line-height: 20px;
	}
	.text-xs {
		font-size: 12px;
		line-height: 16px;
	}
	.text-xxs {
		font-size: 10px;
		line-height: 16px;
	}









	button a {
		text-decoration: none;
		color: inherit;
	}
	blockquote {
		font-size: 23px;
		letter-spacing: -0.3px;
		line-height: 34px;
	}
	button {
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
		font-size: 12px;
		letter-spacing: 0;
		line-height: 15px;
		text-align: center;
		padding: 15px;
	}
	input, textarea {
		padding: 5px 10px;
		border: 1px solid var(--gray-300);
		display: block;
		font-size: 16px;
	}
	label {
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
		font-size: 14px;
		display: inline-block;
		padding-bottom: 5px;
	}
	iframe {
		max-width: 100%;
	}
	img {
		vertical-align: middle;
		max-width: 100%;
		height: auto;
	}
	ul, ol {
		padding: 0 0 0 20px;
		margin: 10px 0;
		line-height: 1.6;
	}

	ol li {
		padding-left: 10px;
	}

	hr {
		margin: 50px 0;
		border: 1px solid #f0f0f0;
	}

	.error-message {
		color: var(--error);
		padding-top: 10px;
		font-size: 13px;
		font-weight: bold;
	}

	/* Make clicks pass-through */
	#nprogress {
		pointer-events: none;
	}

	#nprogress .bar {
		background: #337fbd;

		position: fixed;
		z-index: 1031;
		top: 0;
		left: 0;

		width: 100%;
		height: 2px;
	}

	/* Fancy blur effect */
	#nprogress .peg {
		display: block;
		position: absolute;
		right: 0px;
		width: 100px;
		height: 100%;
		box-shadow: 0 0 10px #337fbd, 0 0 5px #337fbd;
		opacity: 1;

		-webkit-transform: rotate(3deg) translate(0px, -4px);
		-ms-transform: rotate(3deg) translate(0px, -4px);
		transform: rotate(3deg) translate(0px, -4px);
	}

	/* Remove these to get rid of the spinner */
	#nprogress .spinner {
		display: block;
		position: fixed;
		z-index: 1031;
		top: 15px;
		right: 15px;
	}

	#nprogress .spinner-icon {
		width: 18px;
		height: 18px;
		box-sizing: border-box;

		border: solid 2px transparent;
		border-top-color: #29d;
		border-left-color: #29d;
		border-radius: 50%;

		-webkit-animation: nprogress-spinner 400ms linear infinite;
		animation: nprogress-spinner 400ms linear infinite;
	}

	.nprogress-custom-parent {
		overflow: hidden;
		position: relative;
	}

	.nprogress-custom-parent #nprogress .spinner,
	.nprogress-custom-parent #nprogress .bar {
		position: absolute;
	}
	.bcFloat,
	.widget-floating__teaser {
		display: none !important;
	}
	@-webkit-keyframes nprogress-spinner {
		0% {
			-webkit-transform: rotate(0deg);
		}
		100% {
			-webkit-transform: rotate(360deg);
		}
	}
	@keyframes nprogress-spinner {
		0% {
			transform: rotate(0deg);
		}
		100% {
			transform: rotate(360deg);
		}
	}

	/* font_awesome styles */
	.fa,.fab,.fal,.far,.fas {
		-moz-osx-font-smoothing: grayscale;
		-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;
		display: inline-block;
		font-style: normal;
		font-variant: normal;
		text-rendering: auto;
		line-height: 1
	}

Residential","content":"\u003cp\u003eWith \u003ca href=\"/rehab-guide/inpatient\"\u003einpatient residential treatment\u003c/a\u003e, patients live in the treatment hospital or facility 24/7 while receiving supervised and structured care. The \u003ca href=\"/rehab-guide/how-long-rehab-last\"\u003elength of treatment\u003c/a\u003e varies according to the level of care needed. But options range from a few days to 30-, 60-, or 90-day stays or longer.\u003c/p\u003e\n"},{"title":"Outpatient Treatment","content":"\u003cp\u003e\u003ca href=\"/rehab-guide/outpatient-treatment\"\u003eOutpatient treatment\u003c/a\u003e doesn’t require overnight stays, so patients live in their own residences and visit hospitals or facilities for \u003ca href=\"/therapy-treatment\"\u003etreatment\u003c/a\u003e. Standard outpatient programs typically involve fewer than 9 hours per week. Robust outpatient options include partial hospitalization (PHP) (aka day treatment) and \u003ca href=\"/intensive-outpatient-programs\"\u003eintensive outpatient (IOP)\u003c/a\u003e care.\u003c/p\u003e\n"},{"title":"Safe Medical Detox","content":"\u003cp\u003eWhile not always required, \u003ca href=\"/drug-detox\"\u003emedical detox\u003c/a\u003e aims to medically stabilize patients, prevent harmful effects of withdrawal, minimize withdrawal symptoms, and help patients transition to a rehabilitation program or other form of continued care.\u003c/p\u003e\n"},{"title":"Sober Living","content":"\u003cp\u003eOur \u003ca href=\"/sober-living\"\u003esober living facilities\u003c/a\u003e offer drug- and alcohol-free living environments that help residents solidify habits and coping mechanisms before returning to everyday life.\u003c/p\u003e\n"},{"title":"Co-Occurring Disorders","content":"\u003cp\u003e\u003ca href=\"/rehab-guide/substance-use-disorder\"\u003eSubstance use disorders (SUDs)\u003c/a\u003e often occur alongside mental disorders, such as anxiety, depression, post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), and more. Our facilities treat the whole person, offering care for both SUDs and \u003ca href=\"/co-occurring-disorders\"\u003eco-occurring disorders\u003c/a\u003e.\u003c/p\u003e\n"},{"title":"Specialized Programs","content":"\u003cp\u003eGiven the importance of individualized treatment, we provide a host of programs geared toward various patient groups such as \u003ca href=\"/veterans/specialized-treatment-program\"\u003eVeterans and first responders\u003c/a\u003e and members of the \u003ca href=\"/lgbtqiapk-addiction/lgbtq-drug-addiction-substance-abuse\"\u003eLGBTQ+\u003c/a\u003e community. Plus, AAC provides not only faith-based and 12-step programs such as Alcoholics Anonymous and Narcotics Anonymous but also secular program options. In addition, we offer a special opportunity for \u003ca href=\"https://aacalumni.com/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003ealumni\u003c/a\u003e to connect, support one another, and contribute to the community.\u003c/p\u003e\n"},{"title":"Therapies","content":"\u003cp\u003eGroup, one-on-one, and \u003ca href=\"/therapy-treatment/family-therapy\"\u003efamily therapies\u003c/a\u003e are available as are trauma-based therapies to help individuals address underlying issues that can contribute to substance misuse and addiction. Specific therapy types include: \u003ca href=\"/therapy-treatment/cognitive-behavioral-therapy\"\u003ecognitive behavioral therapy\u003c/a\u003e, \u003ca href=\"/addiction-medications\"\u003emedication assisted treatment\u003c/a\u003e, contingency management, \u003ca href=\"/therapy-treatment/motivational-interviewing\"\u003emotivational interviewing\u003c/a\u003e, and much more.\u003c/p\u003e\n"},{"title":"Telehealth","content":"\u003cp\u003eOffered via confidential, HIPAA-compliant video conferences, our outpatient telehealth services provide online treatment support. Services are available to residents of Arizona, Florida, Missouri, Nevada, Pennsylvania, Rhode Island, Texas, Vermont, and Washington.\u003c/p\u003e\n"}],"homepage_customizations":{"link_bank":[{"title":"Paying for Treatment","links":[{"copy":"Payment Options","href":"/payment-options"},{"copy":"Check Your Insurance Coverage","href":"/verify-insurance"},{"copy":"Insurance Provider Information","href":"/insurance-coverage"}]},{"title":"Treatment Resources","links":[{"copy":"Rehab Guide for Addiction","href":"/rehab-guide"},{"copy":"Veteran Treatment Resources ","href":"/veterans"},{"copy":"State-Funded Rehab","href":"/rehab-guide/state-funded"}]},{"title":"FAQs","links":[{"copy":"Our Admission Process","href":"/admissions"},{"copy":"Rehab Cost","href":"/rehab-guide/rehab-cost"},{"copy":"Length of Stay","href":"/rehab-guide/how-long-rehab-last"}]}]}}},"acfOptions":{"acf":{"content_segment_cta_defaults":{"":null,"segment-pr-1":{"value_prop_cta":{"title":"Struggling?  What to expect? 

 
 

 

EXPERT ADDICTION TREATMENT

15+ Years of Trusted Care

 

At AAC, we treat drug and alcohol addiction along with mental and behavioral health issues at multiple locations nationwide.

 View Our Locations 

 Outpatient Locations 

 

Personalized Support

Not sure where to start? Follow our guided intake process.

 

Our guided online intake process makes it easy to take the first step, whether it’s for you or someone you care about. You'll answer a few simple questions to help us understand your unique situation and connect you with the right treatment options. It’s quick, simple, and personalized to make getting help as easy as possible.

OUR CONTENT POLICY

Editorial Policy & Content Guidelines

We are committed to delivering original, truthful, accurate, unbiased, up-to-date information. We strive to create content that is clear, concise, and easy to understand. Learn more about our policy .

 
 

Our Policy

AAC does not accept any advertisements or sponsorships. Our pages remain true to current best practices, without the influence of sponsorship or advertising.

 
 
 
 

Our Sources

During the editorial and medical review process, our team fact checks information and ensures that we only use reputable, credible, and current sources in our content.

 
 
 
 
 

Our Contributors

Our editors and medical reviewers include AAC staff and industry experts. Our credentialed editors are addiction experts who review content to ensure that it is accurate and current.

FROM PAST PATIENTS

Alumni Testimonials
 View All Testimonials 
 

 
 

 
 

 
Support Built for Veterans

Our specialized program honors your service with care designed for veterans. Many of our staff have served too, and are here to support your recovery.

 Get Veteran Guide See Our Veteran Staff 

 

OUR TALK SHOW

“Addiction Talk” is an award-winning online talk show featuring the personal stories of celebrities, influencers, and everyday people impacted by addiction.

		line-height: 32px;
	}
	.text-xl {
		font-size: 20px;
		line-height: 32px;
	}
	.text-lg {
		font-size: 18px;
		line-height: 28px;
	}
	.text-base {
		font-size: 16px;
		line-height: 24px;
	}
	.text-sm {
		font-size: 14px;
		line-height: 20px;
	}
	.text-xs {
		font-size: 12px;
		line-height: 16px;
	}
	.text-xxs {
		font-size: 10px;
		line-height: 16px;
	}









	button a {
		text-decoration: none;
		color: inherit;
	}
	blockquote {
		font-size: 23px;
		letter-spacing: -0.3px;
		line-height: 34px;
	}
	button {
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
		font-size: 12px;
		letter-spacing: 0;
		line-height: 15px;
		text-align: center;
		padding: 15px;
	}
	input, textarea {
		padding: 5px 10px;
		border: 1px solid var(--gray-300);
		display: block;
		font-size: 16px;
	}
	label {
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
		font-size: 14px;
		display: inline-block;
		padding-bottom: 5px;
	}
	iframe {
		max-width: 100%;
	}
	img {
		vertical-align: middle;
		max-width: 100%;
		height: auto;
	}
	ul, ol {
		padding: 0 0 0 20px;
		margin: 10px 0;
		line-height: 1.6;
	}

	ol li {
		padding-left: 10px;
	}

	hr {
		margin: 50px 0;
		border: 1px solid #f0f0f0;
	}

	.error-message {
		color: var(--error);
		padding-top: 10px;
		font-size: 13px;
		font-weight: bold;
	}

	/* Make clicks pass-through */
	#nprogress {
		pointer-events: none;
	}

	#nprogress .bar {
		background: #337fbd;

		position: fixed;
		z-index: 1031;
		top: 0;
		left: 0;

		width: 100%;
		height: 2px;
	}

	/* Fancy blur effect */
	#nprogress .peg {
		display: block;
		position: absolute;
		right: 0px;
		width: 100px;
		height: 100%;
		box-shadow: 0 0 10px #337fbd, 0 0 5px #337fbd;
		opacity: 1;

		-webkit-transform: rotate(3deg) translate(0px, -4px);
		-ms-transform: rotate(3deg) translate(0px, -4px);
		transform: rotate(3deg) translate(0px, -4px);
	}

	/* Remove these to get rid of the spinner */
	#nprogress .spinner {
		display: block;
		position: fixed;
		z-index: 1031;
		top: 15px;
		right: 15px;
	}

	#nprogress .spinner-icon {
		width: 18px;
		height: 18px;
		box-sizing: border-box;

		border: solid 2px transparent;
		border-top-color: #29d;
		border-left-color: #29d;
		border-radius: 50%;

		-webkit-animation: nprogress-spinner 400ms linear infinite;
		animation: nprogress-spinner 400ms linear infinite;
	}

	.nprogress-custom-parent {
		overflow: hidden;
		position: relative;
	}

	.nprogress-custom-parent #nprogress .spinner,
	.nprogress-custom-parent #nprogress .bar {
		position: absolute;
	}
	.bcFloat,
	.widget-floating__teaser {
		display: none !important;
	}
	@-webkit-keyframes nprogress-spinner {
		0% {
			-webkit-transform: rotate(0deg);
		}
		100% {
			-webkit-transform: rotate(360deg);
		}
	}
	@keyframes nprogress-spinner {
		0% {
			transform: rotate(0deg);
		}
		100% {
			transform: rotate(360deg);
		}
	}

	/* font_awesome styles */
	.fa,.fab,.fal,.far,.fas {
		-moz-osx-font-smoothing: grayscale;
		-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;
		display: inline-block;
		font-style: normal;
		font-variant: normal;
		text-rendering: auto;
		line-height: 1
	}

	/* latin */
	@font-face {
		font-family: 'Inter';
		font-style: normal;
		font-weight: 400;

		src: local('Inter Regular'), local('Inter-Regular'),
			url('/static/fonts/inter-regular-optimized.woff2') format('woff2'), /* Chrome 26+, Opera 23+, Firefox 39+ */
			url('/static/fonts/inter-regular-optimized.woff') format('woff'); /* Chrome 6+, Firefox 3.6+, IE 9+, Safari 5.1+ */
	}
	/* latin */
	@font-face {
		font-family: 'Inter';
		font-style: normal;
		font-weight: bold;
		font-display: swap;
		src: local('Inter Heavy'), local('Inter-Heavy'),
			url('/static/fonts/inter-bold-optimized.woff2') format('woff2'), /* Chrome 26+, Opera 23+, Firefox 39+ */
			url('/static/fonts/inter-bold-optimized.woff') format('woff'); /* Chrome 6+, Firefox 3.6+, IE 9+, Safari 5.1+ */
	}
	* {
		margin: 0;
		padding: 0;
		box-sizing: border-box;
		-webkit-box-sizing: border-box;
	}
	html, body {
		height: 100%;
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
		color: #1C2838;
		font-size: 16px;
	}
	html {
		height: 100%;
		width: 100%;
		-webkit-font-smoothing: subpixel-antialiased;
	}

	:root {
		--black: #1a1a1a;
		--white: #ffffff;

		--title-font: Inter, -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, Segoe UI, Roboto, Oxygen-Sans, Ubuntu, Cantarell, Helvetica Neue, sans-serif;
		--copy-font: Inter, -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, Segoe UI, Roboto, Oxygen-Sans, Ubuntu, Cantarell, Helvetica Neue, sans-serif;
		--font-base: 16px;
		--font-base-height: 25px;

		--h1-hero-size: 48px;
		--h1-size: 40px;
		--h2-size: 32px;
		--h3-size: 24px;
		--h4-size: 16px;

		—-breakpoint: 992px;
		—-breakpoint-mobile: 420px;
		—-breakpoint-desktop: 1200px;

		--success: #49a147;
		--warning: #ffad0d;
		--error: #e35141;

		--box-shadow: 0px 5px 20px -5px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.3);
		--box-shadow-100: 0px 1px 3px 1px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.15),
		0px 1px 2px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.3);

		--gray-100: #f8f8f8;
		--gray-200: #eeeeee;
		--gray-300: #cccccc;
		--gray-400: #6a6a6a;
		--gray-500: #1a1a1a;

		--primary: #1d2758;
		--primary-100: #e4e5eb;
		--primary-200: #8e93ac;
		--primary-300: #1d2758;
		--primary-400: #151d47;


		--secondary: #eff6f7;
		--secondary-100: #E5EBF5;
		--secondary-200: #92ACD3;
		--secondary-300: #2459A9;
		--secondary-400: #163E8F;

		--tertiary: #2459a9;
		--tertiary-100: #FAFCFD;
		--tertiary-200: #F4F9F9;
		--tertiary-300: #EFF6F7;
		--tertiary-400: #E4EFF1;

		--interactive: #c77124;
		--interactive-100: #f8eee5;
		--interactive-200: #e3b892;
		--interactive-300: #c77124;
		--interactive-400: #ba5e1b;

		--accent-200: #F4F9F9;

		--focus: #005FCC;

		--gradient-100: linear-gradient(183.11deg, rgba(248, 238, 229, 0.4) 2.57%, rgba(229, 235, 245, 0.4) 97.42%);
		--gradient-200: linear-gradient(90deg, #EFF6F7 0.02%, #F8EEE5 99.96%);
	}

	h1 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 36px;
		line-height: 44px;
	}
	h2 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 30px;
		line-height: 40px;
		margin: 30px 0 24px;
	}
	h3 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 24px;
		line-height: 32px;
		margin: 30px 0 24px;
	}
	h4 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 20px;
		line-height: 32px;
	}

	p, span, a {
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
	}
	p {
		font-size: 16px;
		line-height: 25px;
		margin: 26px 0;
	}
	a {
		color: var(--interactive);
	}


	/* Same styles as p but can be used on <div>s */
	.bold{
		font-weight: bold;
	}
	.text-4xl {
		font-size: 36px;
		line-height: 44px;
	}
	.text-3xl {
		font-size: 30px;
		line-height: 40px;
	}
	.text-2xl {
		font-size: 24px;
		line-height: 32px;
	}
	.text-xl {
		font-size: 20px;
		line-height: 32px;
	}
	.text-lg {
		font-size: 18px;
		line-height: 28px;
	}
	.text-base {
		font-size: 16px;
		line-height: 24px;
	}
	.text-sm {
		font-size: 14px;
		line-height: 20px;
	}
	.text-xs {
		font-size: 12px;
		line-height: 16px;
	}
	.text-xxs {
		font-size: 10px;
		line-height: 16px;
	}









	button a {
		text-decoration: none;
		color: inherit;
	}
	blockquote {
		font-size: 23px;
		letter-spacing: -0.3px;
		line-height: 34px;
	}
	button {
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
		font-size: 12px;
		letter-spacing: 0;
		line-height: 15px;
		text-align: center;
		padding: 15px;
	}
	input, textarea {
		padding: 5px 10px;
		border: 1px solid var(--gray-300);
		display: block;
		font-size: 16px;
	}
	label {
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
		font-size: 14px;
		display: inline-block;
		padding-bottom: 5px;
	}
	iframe {
		max-width: 100%;
	}
	img {
		vertical-align: middle;
		max-width: 100%;
		height: auto;
	}
	ul, ol {
		padding: 0 0 0 20px;
		margin: 10px 0;
		line-height: 1.6;
	}

	ol li {
		padding-left: 10px;
	}

	hr {
		margin: 50px 0;
		border: 1px solid #f0f0f0;
	}

	.error-message {
		color: var(--error);
		padding-top: 10px;
		font-size: 13px;
		font-weight: bold;
	}

	/* Make clicks pass-through */
	#nprogress {
		pointer-events: none;
	}

	#nprogress .bar {
		background: #337fbd;

		position: fixed;
		z-index: 1031;
		top: 0;
		left: 0;

		width: 100%;
		height: 2px;
	}

	/* Fancy blur effect */
	#nprogress .peg {
		display: block;
		position: absolute;
		right: 0px;
		width: 100px;
		height: 100%;
		box-shadow: 0 0 10px #337fbd, 0 0 5px #337fbd;
		opacity: 1;

		-webkit-transform: rotate(3deg) translate(0px, -4px);
		-ms-transform: rotate(3deg) translate(0px, -4px);
		transform: rotate(3deg) translate(0px, -4px);
	}

	/* Remove these to get rid of the spinner */
	#nprogress .spinner {
		display: block;
		position: fixed;
		z-index: 1031;
		top: 15px;
		right: 15px;
	}

	#nprogress .spinner-icon {
		width: 18px;
		height: 18px;
		box-sizing: border-box;

		border: solid 2px transparent;
		border-top-color: #29d;
		border-left-color: #29d;
		border-radius: 50%;

		-webkit-animation: nprogress-spinner 400ms linear infinite;
		animation: nprogress-spinner 400ms linear infinite;
	}

	.nprogress-custom-parent {
		overflow: hidden;
		position: relative;
	}

	.nprogress-custom-parent #nprogress .spinner,
	.nprogress-custom-parent #nprogress .bar {
		position: absolute;
	}
	.bcFloat,
	.widget-floating__teaser {
		display: none !important;
	}
	@-webkit-keyframes nprogress-spinner {
		0% {
			-webkit-transform: rotate(0deg);
		}
		100% {
			-webkit-transform: rotate(360deg);
		}
	}
	@keyframes nprogress-spinner {
		0% {
			transform: rotate(0deg);
		}
		100% {
			transform: rotate(360deg);
		}
	}

	/* font_awesome styles */
	.fa,.fab,.fal,.far,.fas {
		-moz-osx-font-smoothing: grayscale;
		-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;
		display: inline-block;
		font-style: normal;
		font-variant: normal;
		text-rendering: auto;
		line-height: 1
	}

block__grid"><div class="jsx-667780475"><h2 class="jsx-667780475 block__title">What are the steps for getting into treatment?</h2> <div class="jsx-667780475 block__intro">If you are unsure where to begin with addiction treatment, we can help. We've outlined the steps you can expect to undergo during the admissions process for American Addiction Centers' (AAC) treatment facilities. However, if you find that an AAC facility is not the right fit for you, your admissions navigator will provide you with additional resources to help you find treatment elsewhere.</div></div><div class="jsx-667780475"><div class="jsx-667780475 block__content"><div class='portal_accordion' id='portal_accordion-69a75119636dc'><script crossorigin type='application/json'>{"props":{"title":"Step 1: Tell us your story."},"children":"<p><span class=\"s1\"><div class=\"flex-grid-69a7511963653\" style=\"\">\n\t\t<div style=\"flex: 1; text-align: ;\">\n\t\t<\/span><\/p>\n<h3 id=\"tellus\">Step 1: Tell us your story.<\/h3>\n<p class=\"p1\"><span class=\"s1\">Our admissions navigators are available 24\/7 to speak with you. This is your opportunity to tell us your story and what has brought you to the point of picking up the phone. Many of our navigators are in <span class='portal_tooltip' id='portal_tooltip-1'><script crossorigin type='application\/json'>{\"props\":{\"text\":\"Recovery is the process of change through which an individual improves their physical health and wellness, lives a self-directed life, and strives to reach their full substance-free potential.\"},\"children\":\"recovery\"}<\/script><\/span>, so they can truly empathize with what you are going through. Give us a call <span class='portal_phone_link' id='portal_phone_link-69a75119636ad'><script crossorigin type='application\/json'>{\"props\":{\"helpline\":\"true\"},\"children\":\"\"}<\/script><\/span>. See <a href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/admissions\/helpline\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">what to expect when you call<\/a>.<\/span><\/p>\n<p class=\"p1\"><span class=\"s1\">\n\t<\/div>\n\t<\/span><\/p>\n<p><span class=\"s1\"><div style=\"flex: 1; text-align: ;\">\n\t\t<div class='portal_media' id='portal_media-0'><script crossorigin type='application\/json'>{\"props\":{\"image\":\"https:\\\/\\\/admin.americanaddictioncenters.org\\\/wp-content\\\/uploads\\\/2020\\\/11\\\/tellusyourstory8-1.png\"},\"children\":\"\"}<\/script>\n\t<style>\n\t#portal_media-0 {\n\t\tfloat: none;\n\t\twidth: 100%;\n\t\tmargin: 0 auto;\n\t}\n\t<\/style>\n\t<\/div>\n\t<\/div>\n\t\n\t<\/div>\n\t<style>\n\t.flex-grid-69a7511963653 {\n\t\tdisplay: block;\n\t\tmargin: 15px 0;\n\t}\n\t@media screen and (min-width: 1100px) {\n\t\t.flex-grid-69a7511963653 {\n\t\t\tdisplay: flex;\n\t\t\tflex-direction: ;\n\t\t\talign-items: ;\n\t\t\tmargin: 15px 0 30px;\n\t\t}\n\t\t.flex-grid-69a7511963653 > div {\n\t\t\tmargin: 0 15px;\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\t<\/style><\/span><\/p>\n<p><span class=\"s1\"><\/p>\n"}</script></div></span><div class='portal_accordion' id='portal_accordion-69a7511963795'><script crossorigin type='application/json'>{"props":{"title":"Step 2: Complete pre-screening."},"children":"<\/p>\n<p><span class=\"s1\"><div class=\"flex-grid-69a751196374e\" style=\"\">\n\t\t<div style=\"flex: 1; text-align: ;\">\n\t\t<\/span><\/p>\n<h3 id=\"complete\">Step 2: Complete pre-screening.<\/h3>\n<p>During your pre-screening, your navigator will develop an understanding of your clinical needs and the logistics involved with helping you attend treatment. You will be asked questions regarding your mental state, physical limitations, and any medical preconditions you may have. At this time, you will also discuss payment by insurance or another payment method.\n\t<\/div>\n\t<\/p>\n<p class=\"c-mrkdwn__pre\" data-stringify-type=\"pre\"><div style=\"flex: 1; text-align: ;\">\n\t\t<div class='portal_media' id='portal_media-1'><script crossorigin type='application\/json'>{\"props\":{\"image\":\"https:\\\/\\\/admin.americanaddictioncenters.org\\\/wp-content\\\/uploads\\\/2020\\\/11\\\/completescreening8.png\"},\"children\":\"\"}<\/script>\n\t<style>\n\t#portal_media-1 {\n\t\tfloat: none;\n\t\twidth: 100%;\n\t\tmargin: 0 auto;\n\t}\n\t<\/style>\n\t<\/div>\n\t<\/div>\n\t\n\t<\/div>\n\t<style>\n\t.flex-grid-69a751196374e {\n\t\tdisplay: block;\n\t\tmargin: 15px 0;\n\t}\n\t@media screen and (min-width: 1100px) {\n\t\t.flex-grid-69a751196374e {\n\t\t\tdisplay: flex;\n\t\t\tflex-direction: ;\n\t\t\talign-items: ;\n\t\t\tmargin: 15px 0 30px;\n\t\t}\n\t\t.flex-grid-69a751196374e > div {\n\t\t\tmargin: 0 15px;\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\t<\/style><\/p>\n<p data-stringify-type=\"pre\"><span class=\"s1\"><\/p>\n"}</script></div><div class='portal_accordion' id='portal_accordion-69a7511963836'><script crossorigin type='application/json'>{"props":{"title":"Step 3: Receive a personalized treatment recommendation."},"children":"<p><\/span><\/p>\n<p class=\"c-mrkdwn__pre\" data-stringify-type=\"pre\"><div class=\"flex-grid-69a7511963807\" style=\"\">\n\t\t<div style=\"flex: 1; text-align: ;\">\n\t\t<\/p>\n<h3 id=\"receive\" class=\"c-mrkdwn__pre\" data-stringify-type=\"pre\"><strong>Step 3: Receive a personalized treatment recommendation.<\/strong><\/h3>\n<p>Based on your individual circumstances and clinical needs, your navigator will provide you a determination of which AAC facility is the best option for you. You will review it together and the navigator will answer your questions.\n\t<\/div>\n\t<\/p>\n<p class=\"c-mrkdwn__pre\" data-stringify-type=\"pre\"><div style=\"flex: 1; text-align: ;\">\n\t\t<div class='portal_media' id='portal_media-2'><script crossorigin type='application\/json'>{\"props\":{\"image\":\"https:\\\/\\\/admin.americanaddictioncenters.org\\\/wp-content\\\/uploads\\\/2020\\\/11\\\/personalizedtreatment8.png\"},\"children\":\"\"}<\/script>\n\t<style>\n\t#portal_media-2 {\n\t\tfloat: none;\n\t\twidth: 100%;\n\t\tmargin: 0 auto;\n\t}\n\t<\/style>\n\t<\/div>\n\t<\/div>\n\t\n\t<\/div>\n\t<style>\n\t.flex-grid-69a7511963807 {\n\t\tdisplay: block;\n\t\tmargin: 15px 0;\n\t}\n\t@media screen and (min-width: 1100px) {\n\t\t.flex-grid-69a7511963807 {\n\t\t\tdisplay: flex;\n\t\t\tflex-direction: ;\n\t\t\talign-items: ;\n\t\t\tmargin: 15px 0 30px;\n\t\t}\n\t\t.flex-grid-69a7511963807 > div {\n\t\t\tmargin: 0 15px;\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\t<\/style><\/p>\n<p class=\"c-mrkdwn__pre\" data-stringify-type=\"pre\"><span class=\"s1\"><\/p>\n"}</script></div><div class='portal_accordion' id='portal_accordion-69a75119638d3'><script crossorigin type='application/json'>{"props":{"title":"Step 4: Plan your trip with guidance from us."},"children":"<p><\/span><\/p>\n<p class=\"c-mrkdwn__pre\" data-stringify-type=\"pre\"><div class=\"flex-grid-69a75119638a5\" style=\"\">\n\t\t<div style=\"flex: 1; text-align: ;\">\n\t\t<\/p>\n<h3 id=\"plan\" class=\"c-mrkdwn__pre\" data-stringify-type=\"pre\"><b>Step 4: Plan your trip with guidance from us.<\/b><\/h3>\n<p>Once you have reviewed your treatment plan and agreed upon it, it\u2019s time to attend treatment. We will schedule your admission, provide you a packing list, a travel schedule, and any assistance you may need to get to treatment successfully.\n\t<\/div>\n\t<\/p>\n<p class=\"c-mrkdwn__pre\" data-stringify-type=\"pre\"><div style=\"flex: 1; text-align: ;\">\n\t\t<div class='portal_media' id='portal_media-3'><script crossorigin type='application\/json'>{\"props\":{\"image\":\"https:\\\/\\\/admin.americanaddictioncenters.org\\\/wp-content\\\/uploads\\\/2020\\\/11\\\/planyourtrip9.png\"},\"children\":\"\"}<\/script>\n\t<style>\n\t#portal_media-3 {\n\t\tfloat: none;\n\t\twidth: 100%;\n\t\tmargin: 0 auto;\n\t}\n\t<\/style>\n\t<\/div>\n\t<\/div>\n\t\n\t<\/div>\n\t<style>\n\t.flex-grid-69a75119638a5 {\n\t\tdisplay: block;\n\t\tmargin: 15px 0;\n\t}\n\t@media screen and (min-width: 1100px) {\n\t\t.flex-grid-69a75119638a5 {\n\t\t\tdisplay: flex;\n\t\t\tflex-direction: ;\n\t\t\talign-items: ;\n\t\t\tmargin: 15px 0 30px;\n\t\t}\n\t\t.flex-grid-69a75119638a5 > div {\n\t\t\tmargin: 0 15px;\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\t<\/style><\/p>\n<p data-stringify-type=\"pre\"><span class=\"s1\"><\/p>\n"}</script></div><div class='portal_accordion' id='portal_accordion-69a7511963979'><script crossorigin type='application/json'>{"props":{"title":"Step 5: Arrive at selected facility and begin your recovery process."},"children":"<p><\/span><\/p>\n<p class=\"c-mrkdwn__pre\" data-stringify-type=\"pre\"><div class=\"flex-grid-69a751196394b\" style=\"\">\n\t\t<div style=\"flex: 1; </div></div></div></div></div><div id="block-2" class="jsx-1029659034 scroll-block"><div class="sc-7507fce7-0 inyqkz"><div class="jsx-1029659034 block__container"><div class="jsx-667780475 block"><div class="jsx-667780475 block__grid"><div class="jsx-667780475"><h2 class="jsx-667780475 block__title">What to Expect</h2> <div class="jsx-667780475 block__intro">The admissions process can seem daunting at first, but we'll be with you every step of the way to answer any questions and make you feel as comfortable as possible.</div></div><div class="jsx-667780475"><div class="jsx-667780475 block__content"><p><div class='portal_video' id='portal_video-69a7511965544'><script crossorigin type='application/json'>{"props":{"id":"2TZF2ypMeeA","type":"youtube","title":"Our Admissions Process"}}</script></div><br />
<div class='portal_accordion' id='portal_accordion-69a7511965819'><script crossorigin type='application/json'>{"props":{"title":"Family and Friends: What to Expect"},"children":"<\/p>\n<h2>Family and Friends: What to Expect<\/h2>\n<p>Your support can be an invaluable asset in helping your friend or family member get into rehab. However, they may not take the steps you want them to take right away. Don\u2019t let that frustrate you. Continue to encourage them, avoid judgment, and stay committed to helping them get the help they need.<\/p>\n<h3>The Admissions Process<\/h3>\n<p>You can call AAC on their behalf and talk to one of our experienced and compassionate admissions navigators (many of whom are in <span class=\"s1\"><span class='portal_tooltip' id='portal_tooltip-2'><script crossorigin type='application\/json'>{\"props\":{\"text\":\"Recovery is the process of change through which an individual improves their physical health and wellness, lives a self-directed life, and strives to reach their full substance-free potential.\"},\"children\":\"recovery\"}<\/script><\/span><\/span>\u00a0themselves) to begin the admissions <a href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/admissions\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"><u>process<\/u><\/a>. They can help you understand the treatment options that may fit your loved one\u2019s specific needs and even see if treatment may be covered using their insurance\u2014if you know the company, type, and group number on the insurance they carry.<\/p>\n<p>If you are supporting your loved one, who is making the call themselves, encourage them to tell the admissions navigator their story thoroughly and honestly and share why they\u2019ve decided to seek treatment. If your loved one permits it, remain with them while they make the call. The call\u2014the first step in the admissions process\u2014is often a hard hurdle to get over, and your physical presence will remind your loved one that they are not in this alone.<\/p>\n<h3>Before Treatment<\/h3>\n<p>Your loved one might be nervous, anxious, and scared about entering treatment. Assuage their distress by helping them prepare for treatment. Some of the things that you can do include:<\/p>\n<ul>\n<li>Talk to your loved one about their treatment goals. What are their long-term recovery goals? This will help you better support them in their recovery after they complete treatment as well.<\/li>\n<li>Help them get ready. Depending on the program they are entering, they may need comfortable clothing and toiletries if they\u2019re attending an inpatient or residential program, for instance.<\/li>\n<li>Build their support system. Your loved one may need more support during treatment, and you might, too. Enlist the help of family and friends to help with the practical tasks, such as childcare, house cleaning, pet sitting, and providing rides when necessary.<\/li>\n<li>Be there for them. Be supportive and encouraging.<\/li>\n<\/ul>\n<h3>During Treatment<\/h3>\n<p>While you may or may not be able to visit your loved one in an inpatient or residential treatment facility\u2014aside from family therapy sessions\u2014staying in touch with them during treatment can be an important component in their recovery.<\/p>\n<p>Some facilities don\u2019t allow communication immediately upon entry to treatment but then allow phone calls and other forms of electronic communication outside of treatment hours.<\/p>\n<p>On the other hand, actively supporting your loved one may sometimes mean giving them the time and space they need to do the hard work necessary to heal.<\/p>\n<p>Additionally, rehab and recovery can be difficult. If your loved one is in an inpatient facility, they may find many reasons to leave treatment\u2014and may even ask for your help to do so. Don’t give in. Remind them that staying in treatment for the entire duration gives them the best chance at long-lasting recovery.<\/p>\n<p>Finally, it\u2019s important to take care of yourself as well. You can find Al-Anon, Narc-Anon, and other family support groups near you.<\/p>\n<h3>After Treatment<\/h3>\n<p>Once treatment ends, your support is paramount. It\u2019s not the time to seek retribution for past actions.<\/p>\n<p>You can help and encourage them after treatment in a number of ways, including:<\/p>\n<ul>\n<li>Working with them to find ways to remain healthy and substance free.<\/li>\n<li>Eliminating the triggers that can lead them back to addiction.<\/li>\n<li>Helping them get to ongoing therapy sessions, mutual-help meetings, job interviews, doctor\u2019s visits, and other important appointments.<\/li>\n<li>Being respectful of their boundaries. That means no hovering. Remember that sobriety is a choice they need to make for themselves each and every day. You cannot do it for them.<\/li>\n<li>Setting your own boundaries, and communicating behaviors and actions that will not be tolerated.<\/li>\n<li>Offering emotional support in whatever capacity they need\u2014an ear to listen or a shoulder to cry on, for example. Be compassionate and non-judgmental to the feelings and emotions they experience.<\/li>\n<\/ul>\n<h3>When and Why Seek Help<\/h3>\n<p>Addiction is a treatable disease, and it can happen to anyone. In fact, 1 in 7 Americans report experiencing a substance use disorder, the clinical term for addiction.<sup>1 <\/sup><\/p>\n<p>While only a healthcare professional can diagnose a substance use disorder utilizing the criteria outlined in the <em>Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders <\/em>(DSM-5), it may be helpful for friends and family to be aware of the criteria used so they can spot the signs of a potential problem. The criteria clinicians use includes:<sup>2<\/sup><\/p>\n<ul>\n<li>Taking more of a substance than intended, or for longer periods than intended.<\/li>\n<li>Having a persistent desire to cut back or stop using a substance but being unable to do so.<\/li>\n<li>Spending an increasing amount of time obtaining, using, or recovering from substance use.<\/li>\n<li>Experiencing cravings or intense urges to use a certain substance or substances.<\/li>\n<li>Failing to fulfill responsibilities at home, school, or work due to substance use.<\/li>\n<li>Continuing to use the substance despite having persistent social or relational problems that are caused by or worsened by use.<\/li>\n<li>Giving up previously important or meaningful activities or commitments due to substance use.<\/li>\n<li>Repeatedly using the substance in physically dangerous situations.<\/li>\n<li>Continuing to use the substance despite knowing that it causes or worsens ongoing physical or psychological problems.<\/li>\n<li>Developing a tolerance to the substance, which means they require more of the drug to achieve the desired effect or the effect is greatly reduced when using the same dose.<\/li>\n<li>Experiencing withdrawal symptoms when drastically cutting back or trying to abruptly stop substance use.<\/li>\n<\/ul>\n<p>If your loved one experiences 2 or more of these symptoms in a 12-month period, they meet the diagnostic criteria for a substance use disorder.<sup>2<\/sup><\/p>\n<p>You can help them get the treatment they need that is specific to them, their substance use history, and overall needs and treatment goals. A comprehensive treatment plan may include:<sup>3<\/sup><\/p>\n<ul>\n<li>Behavioral interventions to help them change their thoughts, emotions, and behaviors surrounding their substance use.<\/li>\n<li>Continued assessment so treatment can be modified to meet their changing needs.<\/li>\n<li>A variety of services to address their physical, mental, and emotional health.<\/li>\n<li>Coping skills training to help them identify triggers, manage stressors, and prevent future relapse.<\/li>\n<li>Substance use education.<\/li>\n<li>Services such as occupational training and housing help.<\/li>\n<li>Aftercare or long-term, follow-up services such as mutual-help groups or sober living environments to help them avoid relapse.<\/li>\n<\/ul>\n<p>Recovery is a lifelong process\u2014as is your support and encouragement along the way.<\/p>\n<p>\n"}</script></div><div class='portal_accordion' id='portal_accordion-69a7511965ae6'><script crossorigin type='application/json'>{"props":{"title":"Spouse and Significant Others: What to Expect"},"children":"<\/p>\n<h2>Spouse and Significant Others: What to Expect<\/h2>\n<p>Your support can be an invaluable asset in helping your partner get into rehab. Continue to encourage them to seek treatment to care for their health, improve your relationship, and ultimately, stop the cycle of substance use and addiction.<\/p>\n<h3>The Admissions Process<\/h3>\n<p>You can call AAC on their behalf and talk to one of our experienced and compassionate admissions navigators (many of whom are in <span class=\"s1\"><span class='portal_tooltip' id='portal_tooltip-3'><script crossorigin type='application\/json'>{\"props\":{\"text\":\"Recovery is the process of change through which an individual improves their physical health and wellness, lives a self-directed life, and strives to reach their full substance-free potential.\"},\"children\":\"recovery\"}<\/script><\/span><\/span>\u00a0themselves) to begin the admissions <a href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/admissions\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"><u>process<\/u><\/a>. If your spouse permits it, remain with them while they make the call. The call\u2014the first step in the admissions process\u2014is often a hard hurdle to get over, and your physical presence will remind your partner that they are not in this alone.<\/p>\n<h3>Before Treatment<\/h3>\n<p>Your partner might be nervous, anxious, and scared about entering treatment. You can find Al-Anon, Narc-Anon, and other family support groups near you.<\/p>\n<h3>After Treatment<\/h3>\n<p>Once treatment ends, your support is paramount. It\u2019s not the time to seek retribution for past actions.<\/p>\n<p>You can help and encourage your partner after treatment in a number of ways, including:<\/p>\n<ul>\n<li>Working with them to find ways to remain healthy and substance free.<\/li>\n<li>Eliminating the triggers that can lead them back to addiction.<\/li>\n<li>Helping them get to ongoing therapy sessions, mutual-help meetings, job interviews, doctor\u2019s visits, and other important appointments.<\/li>\n<li>Being respectful of their boundaries. The criteria clinicians use includes:<sup>2<\/sup><\/p>\n<ul>\n<li>Taking more of a substance than intended, or for longer periods than intended.<\/li>\n<li>Having a persistent desire to cut back or stop using a substance but being unable to do so.<\/li>\n<li>Spending an increasing amount of time obtaining, using, or recovering from substance use.<\/li>\n<li>Experiencing cravings or intense urges to use a certain substance or substances.<\/li>\n<li>Failing to fulfill responsibilities at home, school, or work due to substance use.<\/li>\n<li>Continuing to use the substance despite having persistent social or relational problems that are caused by or worsened by use.<\/li>\n<li>Giving up previously important or meaningful activities or commitments due to substance use.<\/li>\n<li>Repeatedly using the substance in physically dangerous situations.<\/li>\n<li>Continuing to use the substance despite knowing that it causes or worsens ongoing physical or psychological problems.<\/li>\n<li>Developing a tolerance to the substance, which means they require more of the drug to achieve the desired effect or the effect is greatly reduced when using the same dose.<\/li>\n<li>Experiencing withdrawal symptoms when drastically cutting back or trying to abruptly stop substance use.<\/li>\n<\/ul>\n<p>If your partner experiences 2 or more of these symptoms in a 12-month period, they meet the diagnostic criteria for a substance use disorder.<sup>2<\/sup><\/p>\n<p>You can help them get the treatment they need that is specific to them, their substance use history, and overall needs and treatment goals. A comprehensive treatment plan may include:<sup>3<\/sup><\/p>\n<ul>\n<li>Behavioral interventions to help them change their thoughts, emotions, and behaviors surrounding their substance use.<\/li>\n<li>Continued assessment so treatment can be modified to meet their changing needs.<\/li>\n<li>A variety of services to address their physical, mental, and emotional health.<\/li>\n<li>Coping skills training to help them identify triggers, manage stressors, and prevent future relapse.<\/li>\n<li>Substance use education.<\/li>\n<li>Services such as occupational training and housing help.<\/li>\n<li>Aftercare or long-term, follow-up services such as mutual-help groups or sober living environments to help them avoid relapse.<\/li>\n<\/ul>\n<p>Recovery is a lifelong process\u2014as is your support and encouragement along the way.<\/p>\n<p>\n"}</script></div><div class='portal_accordion' id='portal_accordion-69a7511965da9'><script crossorigin type='application/json'>{"props":{"title":"Parents: What to Expect"},"children":"<\/p>\n<h2>Parents: What to Expect<\/h2>\n<p>Your support can be an invaluable asset in helping your adult child (AAC does not treat individuals under the age of 18) get into rehab. However, your child may not take the steps you want them to take right away, which can be painful and frustrating. Don\u2019t get discouraged. Getting them into treatment can take several attempts. Continue to show your love and concern, avoid judgment, and stay committed to helping them recognize that they need help.<\/p>\n<h3>The Admissions Process<\/h3>\n<p>You can call AAC on behalf of your child and talk to one of our experienced and compassionate admissions navigators (many of whom are in <span class=\"s1\"><span class='portal_tooltip' id='portal_tooltip-4'><script crossorigin type='application\/json'>{\"props\":{\"text\":\"Recovery is the process of change through which an individual improves their physical health and wellness, lives a self-directed life, and strives to reach their full substance-free potential.\"},\"children\":\"recovery\"}<\/script><\/span><\/span>\u00a0themselves) to begin the admissions <a href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/admissions\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"><u>process<\/u><\/a>. The admissions navigator can help you understand the treatment options that may fit your child\u2019s specific needs and even verify if their health insurance covers all or part of substance use treatment.<\/p>\n<p>If you are supporting your child, who is making the call themselves, encourage them to tell the admissions navigator their story thoroughly and honestly and share why they\u2019ve decided to seek treatment. If your child permits it, remain with them while they make the call. The call\u2014the first step in the admissions process\u2014is often a hard hurdle to get over, and your physical presence will remind them that they are not in this alone.<\/p>\n<h3>Before Treatment<\/h3>\n<p>Your child might be nervous, anxious, and scared about entering treatment. Assuage their distress by helping them prepare for treatment. Some of the things that you can do include:<\/p>\n<ul>\n<li>Talk to your child about their treatment goals. What are their long-term recovery goals? This will help you better support them in their recovery after they complete treatment as well.<\/li>\n<li>Help them get ready. Depending on the program they are entering, they may need comfortable clothing and toiletries if they\u2019re attending an inpatient or residential program, for instance.<\/li>\n<li>Be there for them. Maintain a positive and affirming attitude about their recovery and provide ongoing support.<\/li>\n<\/ul>\n<h3>During Treatment<\/h3>\n<p>While you may or may not be able to visit your child in an inpatient or residential treatment facility\u2014aside from family therapy sessions or specific family events\u2014staying in touch with them during treatment can be an important component in their overall recovery.<\/p>\n<p>Some facilities don\u2019t allow communication immediately upon entry to treatment but then allow phone calls and other forms of electronic communication outside of treatment hours.<\/p>\n<p>On the other hand, actively supporting your child may sometimes mean giving them the time and space they need to do the hard work necessary to heal.<\/p>\n<p>Additionally, rehab and recovery can be difficult. If your child is in an inpatient facility, they may find many reasons to leave treatment\u2014and may even ask for your help to do so. Don\u2019t give in. Remind them that staying in treatment for the entire duration gives them the best chance at long-lasting recovery.<\/p>\n<p>Finally, it\u2019s important to take care of yourself as well. You can find Al-Anon, Narc-Anon, and other family support groups near you.<\/p>\n<h3>After Treatment<\/h3>\n<p>Once treatment ends, your support is paramount.<\/p>\n<p>You can help, encourage, and support your child after their formal treatment ends in a number of ways, including:<\/p>\n<ul>\n<li>Working with them to find ways to remain healthy and substance free.<\/li>\n<li>Eliminating the triggers that can lead them back to addiction.<\/li>\n<li>Helping them get to ongoing therapy sessions, mutual-help meetings, job interviews, doctor\u2019s visits, and other important appointments.<\/li>\n<li>Being respectful of their boundaries. That means no hovering. Remember that sobriety is a choice they need to make for themselves each and every day. You cannot do it for them.<\/li>\n<li>Setting your own boundaries, and communicating behaviors and actions that will not be tolerated.<\/li>\n<li>Offering emotional support in whatever capacity they need\u2014an ear to listen or a shoulder to cry on, for example. Be compassionate and non-judgmental to the feelings and emotions they experience.<\/li>\n<\/ul>\n<h3>When and Why Seek Help<\/h3>\n<p>Addiction is a treatable disease, and it can happen to anyone. In fact, 1 in 7 Americans report experiencing a substance use disorder, the clinical term for addiction.<sup>1 <\/sup><\/p>\n<p>While only a healthcare professional can diagnose a substance use disorder utilizing the criteria outlined in the <em>Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders <\/em>(DSM-5), it may be helpful for you to be aware of the criteria used so you can spot the signs of a potential problem. The criteria clinicians use includes:<sup>2<\/sup><\/p>\n<ul>\n<li>Taking more of a substance than intended, or for longer periods than intended.<\/li>\n<li>Having a persistent desire to cut back or stop using a substance but being unable to do so.<\/li>\n<li>Spending an increasing amount of time obtaining, using, or recovering from substance use.<\/li>\n<li>Experiencing cravings or intense urges to use a certain substance or substances.<\/li>\n<li>Failing to fulfill responsibilities at home, school, or work due to substance use.<\/li>\n<li>Continuing to use the substance despite having persistent social or relational problems that are caused by or worsened by use.<\/li>\n<li>Giving up previously important or meaningful activities or commitments due to substance use.<\/li>\n<li>Repeatedly using the substance in physically dangerous situations.<\/li>\n<li>Continuing to use the substance despite knowing that it causes or worsens ongoing physical or psychological problems.<\/li>\n<li>Developing a tolerance to the substance, which means they require more of the drug to achieve the desired effect or the effect is greatly reduced when using the same dose.<\/li>\n<li>Experiencing withdrawal symptoms when drastically cutting back or trying to abruptly stop substance use.<\/li>\n<\/ul>\n<p>If your child experiences 2 or more of these symptoms in a 12-month period, they meet the diagnostic criteria for a substance use disorder.<sup>2<\/sup><\/p>\n<p>You can help them get the treatment they need that is specific to them, their substance use history, and overall needs and treatment goals. A comprehensive treatment plan may include:<sup>3<\/sup><\/p>\n<ul>\n<li>Behavioral interventions to help them change their thoughts, emotions, and behaviors surrounding their substance use.<\/li>\n<li>Continued assessment so treatment can be modified to meet their changing needs.<\/li>\n<li>A variety of services to address their physical, mental, and emotional health.<\/li>\n<li>Coping skills training to help them identify triggers, manage stressors, and prevent future relapse.<\/li>\n<li>Substance use education.<\/li>\n<li>Services such as occupational training and housing help.<\/li>\n<li>Aftercare or long-term, follow-up services such as mutual-help groups or sober living environments to help them avoid relapse.<\/li>\n<\/ul>\n<p>Recovery is a lifelong process\u2014as is your support and encouragement along the way.<\/p>\n<p>\n"}</script></div><div class='portal_accordion' id='portal_accordion-69a7511965f6f'><script crossorigin type='application/json'>{"props":{"title":"Co-Workers: What to Expect"},"children":"<\/p>\n<h2>Co-Workers: What to Expect<\/h2>\n<p>Handling suspected substance misuse with a colleague is very different than attempting to help a family member or friend. First of all, you should objectively document all incidents where you witness behaviors that suggest the individual may be misusing drugs or alcohol.<sup>4 <\/sup>Review your company\u2019s human resources policy to ensure that you bring your documentation to the proper individual\u2014your supervisor or someone in HR, perhaps\u2014who can then determine the proper course of action to get your colleague the help they need.<sup>5<\/sup><\/p>\n<h3>The Admissions Process<\/h3>\n<p>While you may not be part of the admissions process, you can support your co-worker by being there for them and letting them know that you\u2019re keeping their situation completely confidential. In addition, you can remind them about some of the benefits that may be available to them through work\u2014such as an Employee Assistance Program (EAP), which can help employees with personal issues, including drug- and alcohol-related problems. You may also encourage them to look into the Family Medical Leave Act (<a href=\"https:\/\/webapps.dol.gov\/elaws\/whd\/fmla\/10c9.aspx\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">FMLA<\/a>), which allows eligible employees to take up to 12 weeks of unpaid leave to receive treatment for a substance use disorder.<sup>6 <\/sup><\/p>\n<h3>Before Treatment<\/h3>\n<p>Your colleague might be nervous, anxious, and scared about entering treatment. Some of the things that you can do to offer support include:<\/p>\n<ul>\n<li>Offer help. Offer to assist with the practical tasks, such as childcare, house cleaning, pet sitting, and providing rides when necessary.<\/li>\n<li>Be there for them. Be supportive and encouraging.<\/li>\n<\/ul>\n<h3>After Treatment<\/h3>\n<p>It can be hard to know the proper way to behave around a colleague who returns from treatment for a substance use disorder. You should welcome your colleague back but respect their privacy. Don\u2019t pry. Instead, let them decide if and when they want to bring up rehab.<\/p>\n<h3>When and Why Seek Help<\/h3>\n<p>Addiction is a treatable disease, and it can happen to anyone. In fact, 1 in 7 Americans report experiencing a substance use disorder, the clinical term for addiction.<sup>1 <\/sup><\/p>\n<p>While only a healthcare professional can diagnose a substance use disorder utilizing the criteria outlined in the <em>Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders <\/em>(DSM-5), it may be helpful for you to be aware of the criteria used so you can spot the signs of a potential problem. The criteria clinicians use includes:<sup>2<\/sup><\/p>\n<ul>\n<li>Taking more of a substance than intended, or for longer periods than intended.<\/li>\n<li>Having a persistent desire to cut back or stop using a substance but being unable to do so.<\/li>\n<li>Spending an increasing amount of time obtaining, using, or recovering from substance use.<\/li>\n<li>Experiencing cravings or intense urges to use a certain substance or substances.<\/li>\n<li>Failing to fulfill responsibilities at home, school, or work due to substance use.<\/li>\n<li>Continuing to use the substance despite having persistent social or relational problems that are caused by or worsened by use.<\/li>\n<li>Giving up previously important or meaningful activities or commitments due to substance use.<\/li>\n<li>Repeatedly using the substance in physically dangerous situations.<\/li>\n<li>Continuing to use the substance despite knowing that it causes or worsens ongoing physical or psychological problems.<\/li>\n<li>Developing a tolerance to the substance, which means they require more of the drug to achieve the desired effect or the effect is greatly reduced when using the same dose.<\/li>\n<li>Experiencing withdrawal symptoms when drastically cutting back or trying to abruptly stop substance use.<\/li>\n<\/ul>\n<p>If your co-worker experiences 2 or more of these symptoms in a 12-month period, they meet the diagnostic criteria for a substance use disorder.<sup>2<\/sup><\/p>\n<p>You can help them get the treatment they need that is specific to them, their substance use history, and overall needs and treatment goals. A comprehensive treatment plan may include:<sup>3<\/sup><\/p>\n<ul>\n<li>Behavioral interventions to help them change their thoughts, emotions, and behaviors surrounding their substance use.<\/li>\n<li>Continued assessment so treatment can be modified to meet their changing needs.<\/li>\n<li>A variety of services to address their physical, mental, and emotional health.<\/li>\n<li>Coping skills training to help them identify triggers, manage stressors, and prevent future relapse.<\/li>\n<li>Substance use education.<\/li>\n<li>Services such as occupational training and housing help.<\/li>\n<li>Aftercare or long-term, follow-up services such as mutual-help groups or sober living environments to help them avoid relapse.<\/li>\n<\/ul>\n<p>Recovery is a lifelong process\u2014as is your support and encouragement along the way.<\/p>\n<p>\n"}</script></div><div class='portal_accordion' id='portal_accordion-69a7511965fa3'><script crossorigin type='application/json'>{"props":{"title":"Veterans: What to Expect"},"children":"<\/p>\n<p>For information about Veteran admissions, <a href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/admissions\/veterans\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">click here<\/a>.<\/p>\n<p>\n"}</script></div><div class='portal_accordion' id='portal_accordion-69a7511966064'><script crossorigin type='application/json'>{"props":{"title":"Sources"},"children":"<\/p>\n<ol>\n<li>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. type='application/json'>{"props":{"title":"FAQs"},"children":"<\/p>\n<h3>Does my insurance cover treatment?<\/h3>\n<p>AAC works with most <a href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/insurance-providers\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">insurance providers<\/a>. Our admissions navigators will assist you in verifying your specific insurance policy. We are able to guide you through what your coverage looks like at no cost, and the entire process is completely confidential.<\/p>\n<h3>How long will treatment last?<\/h3>\n<p>Depending on the level of treatment and care needed, rehab can last anywhere from 5 to 90 days and beyond. Typically, detox can last between 5 and 7 days, while inpatient and residential programs can last between 30 and 90 days and beyond. AAC focuses on individualized treatment. We offer a full continuum of care with <a href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/drug-detox\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">detox<\/a>, <a href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/rehab-guide\/residential\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">residential<\/a> programs, all levels of <a href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/rehab-guide\/outpatient-treatment\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">outpatient<\/a> care, and <a href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/telehealth\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">telehealth<\/a>. You will be offered all levels of care and treated based on your specific needs. Our <a href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/admissions\/navigator-team\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">admissions navigators<\/a> will assist you in determining what your specific treatment will look like at no cost and no obligation to enter treatment at one of our treatment facilities.<\/p>\n<h3>Will I be uncomfortable during detox?<\/h3>\n<p>AAC’s detox is supervised by nurses and doctors 24\/7 to ensure that you will be medically stable and as comfortable as possible. Our medical team individualizes your detox plan with the use of medications. Our main priority is your safety and comfort as you begin your journey to recovery.<\/p>\n<h3>How quickly can I get admitted into treatment?<\/h3>\n<p>At AAC, you may be able to enter a program immediately in order to medically detox and then move on to a formal treatment program to teach you the coping skills to get you on the road to long-term recovery. If you or a loved one struggle with a substance use disorder or an alcohol use disorder, please connect with one of our <a href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/admissions\/navigator-team\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">admissions navigators<\/a>.<\/p>\n"}</script></div><div class='portal_accordion' id='portal_accordion-69a751196794f'><script crossorigin Process\"}}\u003c/script\u003e\u003c/div\u003e\u003cbr /\u003e\n\u003cdiv class='portal_accordion' id='portal_accordion-69a7511965819'\u003e\u003cscript crossorigin type='application/json'\u003e{\"props\":{\"title\":\"Family and Friends: What to Expect\"},\"children\":\"\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003ch2\u003eFamily and Friends: What to Expect\u003c\\/h2\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eYour support can be an invaluable asset in helping your friend or family member get into rehab. However, they may not take the steps you want them to take right away. Don\\u2019t let that frustrate you. Continue to encourage them, avoid judgment, and stay committed to helping them get the help they need.\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003ch3\u003eThe Admissions Process\u003c\\/h3\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eYou can call AAC on their behalf and talk to one of our experienced and compassionate admissions navigators (many of whom are in \u003cspan class=\\\"s1\\\"\u003e\u003cspan class='portal_tooltip' id='portal_tooltip-2'\u003e\u003cscript crossorigin type='application\\/json'\u003e{\\\"props\\\":{\\\"text\\\":\\\"Recovery is the process of change through which an individual improves their physical health and wellness, lives a self-directed life, and strives to reach their full substance-free potential.\\\"},\\\"children\\\":\\\"recovery\\\"}\u003c\\/script\u003e\u003c\\/span\u003e\u003c\\/span\u003e\\u00a0themselves) to begin the admissions \u003ca href=\\\"https:\\/\\/americanaddictioncenters.org\\/admissions\\\" target=\\\"_blank\\\" rel=\\\"noopener\\\"\u003e\u003cu\u003eprocess\u003c\\/u\u003e\u003c\\/a\u003e. They can help you understand the treatment options that may fit your loved one\\u2019s specific needs and even see if treatment may be covered using their insurance\\u2014if you know the company, type, and group number on the insurance they carry.\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eIf you are supporting your loved one, who is making the call themselves, encourage them to tell the admissions navigator their story thoroughly and honestly and share why they\\u2019ve decided to seek treatment. If your loved one permits it, remain with them while they make the call. The call\\u2014the first step in the admissions process\\u2014is often a hard hurdle to get over, and your physical presence will remind your loved one that they are not in this alone.\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003ch3\u003eBefore Treatment\u003c\\/h3\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eYour loved one might be nervous, anxious, and scared about entering treatment. Assuage their distress by helping them prepare for treatment. Some of the things that you can do include:\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cul\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eTalk to your loved one about their treatment goals. What are their long-term recovery goals? This will help you better support them in their recovery after they complete treatment as well.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eHelp them get ready. Depending on the program they are entering, they may need comfortable clothing and toiletries if they\\u2019re attending an inpatient or residential program, for instance.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eBuild their support system. Your loved one may need more support during treatment, and you might, too. Enlist the help of family and friends to help with the practical tasks, such as childcare, house cleaning, pet sitting, and providing rides when necessary.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eBe there for them. Be supportive and encouraging.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003c\\/ul\u003e\\n\u003ch3\u003eDuring Treatment\u003c\\/h3\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eWhile you may or may not be able to visit your loved one in an inpatient or residential treatment facility\\u2014aside from family therapy sessions\\u2014staying in touch with them during treatment can be an important component in their recovery.\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eSome facilities don\\u2019t allow communication immediately upon entry to treatment but then allow phone calls and other forms of electronic communication outside of treatment hours.\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eOn the other hand, actively supporting your loved one may sometimes mean giving them the time and space they need to do the hard work necessary to heal.\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eAdditionally, rehab and recovery can be difficult. If your loved one is in an inpatient facility, they may find many reasons to leave treatment\\u2014and may even ask for your help to do so. Don\u0026#8217;t give in. Remind them that staying in treatment for the entire duration gives them the best chance at long-lasting recovery.\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eFinally, it\\u2019s important to take care of yourself as well. You can find Al-Anon, Narc-Anon, and other family support groups near you.\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003ch3\u003eAfter Treatment\u003c\\/h3\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eOnce treatment ends, your support is paramount. It\\u2019s not the time to seek retribution for past actions.\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eYou can help and encourage them after treatment in a number of ways, including:\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cul\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eWorking with them to find ways to remain healthy and substance free.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eEliminating the triggers that can lead them back to addiction.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eHelping them get to ongoing therapy sessions, mutual-help meetings, job interviews, doctor\\u2019s visits, and other important appointments.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eBeing respectful of their boundaries. That means no hovering. Remember that sobriety is a choice they need to make for themselves each and every day. You cannot do it for them.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eSetting your own boundaries, and communicating behaviors and actions that will not be tolerated.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eOffering emotional support in whatever capacity they need\\u2014an ear to listen or a shoulder to cry on, for example. Be compassionate and non-judgmental to the feelings and emotions they experience.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003c\\/ul\u003e\\n\u003ch3\u003eWhen and Why Seek Help\u003c\\/h3\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eAddiction is a treatable disease, and it can happen to anyone. In fact, 1 in 7 Americans report experiencing a substance use disorder, the clinical term for addiction.\u003csup\u003e1 \u003c\\/sup\u003e\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eWhile only a healthcare professional can diagnose a substance use disorder utilizing the criteria outlined in the \u003cem\u003eDiagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders \u003c\\/em\u003e(DSM-5), it may be helpful for friends and family to be aware of the criteria used so they can spot the signs of a potential problem. The criteria clinicians use includes:\u003csup\u003e2\u003c\\/sup\u003e\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cul\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eTaking more of a substance than intended, or for longer periods than intended.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eHaving a persistent desire to cut back or stop using a substance but being unable to do so.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eSpending an increasing amount of time obtaining, using, or recovering from substance use.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eExperiencing cravings or intense urges to use a certain substance or substances.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eFailing to fulfill responsibilities at home, school, or work due to substance use.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eContinuing to use the substance despite having persistent social or relational problems that are caused by or worsened by use.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eGiving up previously important or meaningful activities or commitments due to substance use.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eRepeatedly using the substance in physically dangerous situations.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eContinuing to use the substance despite knowing that it causes or worsens ongoing physical or psychological problems.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eDeveloping a tolerance to the substance, which means they require more of the drug to achieve the desired effect or the effect is greatly reduced when using the same dose.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eExperiencing withdrawal symptoms when drastically cutting back or trying to abruptly stop substance use.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003c\\/ul\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eIf your loved one experiences 2 or more of these symptoms in a 12-month period, they meet the diagnostic criteria for a substance use disorder.\u003csup\u003e2\u003c\\/sup\u003e\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eYou can help them get the treatment they need that is specific to them, their substance use history, and overall needs and treatment goals. A comprehensive treatment plan may include:\u003csup\u003e3\u003c\\/sup\u003e\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cul\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eBehavioral interventions to help them change their thoughts, emotions, and behaviors surrounding their substance use.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eContinued assessment so treatment can be modified to meet their changing needs.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eA variety of services to address their physical, mental, and emotional health.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eCoping skills training to help them identify triggers, manage stressors, and prevent future relapse.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eSubstance use education.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eServices such as occupational training and housing help.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eAftercare or long-term, follow-up services such as mutual-help groups or sober living environments to help them avoid relapse.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003c\\/ul\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eRecovery is a lifelong process\\u2014as is your support and encouragement along the way.\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003e\\n\"}\u003c/script\u003e\u003c/div\u003e\u003cdiv class='portal_accordion' id='portal_accordion-69a7511965ae6'\u003e\u003cscript crossorigin type='application/json'\u003e{\"props\":{\"title\":\"Spouse and Significant Others: What to Expect\"},\"children\":\"\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003ch2\u003eSpouse and Significant Others: What to Expect\u003c\\/h2\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eYour support can be an invaluable asset in helping your partner get into rehab. However, they may be reluctant to go if they\\u2019re not ready. Don\\u2019t let that frustrate you. Continue to encourage them to seek treatment to care for their health, improve your relationship, and ultimately, stop the cycle of substance use and addiction.\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003ch3\u003eThe Admissions Process\u003c\\/h3\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eYou can call AAC on their behalf and talk to one of our experienced and compassionate admissions navigators (many of whom are in \u003cspan class=\\\"s1\\\"\u003e\u003cspan class='portal_tooltip' id='portal_tooltip-3'\u003e\u003cscript crossorigin type='application\\/json'\u003e{\\\"props\\\":{\\\"text\\\":\\\"Recovery is the process of change through which an individual improves their physical health and wellness, lives a self-directed life, and strives to reach their full substance-free potential.\\\"},\\\"children\\\":\\\"recovery\\\"}\u003c\\/script\u003e\u003c\\/span\u003e\u003c\\/span\u003e\\u00a0themselves) to begin the admissions \u003ca href=\\\"https:\\/\\/americanaddictioncenters.org\\/admissions\\\" target=\\\"_blank\\\" rel=\\\"noopener\\\"\u003e\u003cu\u003eprocess\u003c\\/u\u003e\u003c\\/a\u003e. They can help you understand the treatment options that may fit your partner\\u2019s specific needs and even verify if their health insurance covers all or part of substance use treatment.\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eIf you are supporting your partner, who is making the call themselves, encourage them to tell the admissions navigator their story thoroughly and honestly and share why they\\u2019ve decided to seek treatment. If your spouse permits it, remain with them while they make the call. The call\\u2014the first step in the admissions process\\u2014is often a hard hurdle to get over, and your physical presence will remind your partner that they are not in this alone.\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003ch3\u003eBefore Treatment\u003c\\/h3\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eYour partner might be nervous, anxious, and scared about entering treatment.
