In fact, 1 in 7 Americans report experiencing a substance use disorder, the clinical term for addiction.\u003csup\u003e1 \u003c\\/sup\u003e\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eWhile only a healthcare professional can diagnose a substance use disorder utilizing the criteria outlined in the \u003cem\u003eDiagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders \u003c\\/em\u003e(DSM-5), it may be helpful for you to be aware of the criteria used so you can spot the signs of a potential problem. The criteria clinicians use includes:\u003csup\u003e2\u003c\\/sup\u003e\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cul\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eTaking more of a substance than intended, or for longer periods than intended.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eHaving a persistent desire to cut back or stop using a substance but being unable to do so.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eSpending an increasing amount of time obtaining, using, or recovering from substance use.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eExperiencing cravings or intense urges to use a certain substance or substances.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eFailing to fulfill responsibilities at home, school, or work due to substance use.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eContinuing to use the substance despite having persistent social or relational problems that are caused by or worsened by use.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eGiving up previously important or meaningful activities or commitments due to substance use.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eRepeatedly using the substance in physically dangerous situations.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eContinuing to use the substance despite knowing that it causes or worsens ongoing physical or psychological problems.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eDeveloping a tolerance to the substance, which means they require more of the drug to achieve the desired effect or the effect is greatly reduced when using the same dose.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eExperiencing withdrawal symptoms when drastically cutting back or trying to abruptly stop substance use.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003c\\/ul\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eIf your child experiences 2 or more of these symptoms in a 12-month period, they meet the diagnostic criteria for a substance use disorder.\u003csup\u003e2\u003c\\/sup\u003e\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eYou can help them get the treatment they need that is specific to them, their substance use history, and overall needs and treatment goals. A comprehensive treatment plan may include:\u003csup\u003e3\u003c\\/sup\u003e\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cul\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eBehavioral interventions to help them change their thoughts, emotions, and behaviors surrounding their substance use.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eContinued assessment so treatment can be modified to meet their changing needs.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eA variety of services to address their physical, mental, and emotional health.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eCoping skills training to help them identify triggers, manage stressors, and prevent future relapse.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eSubstance use education.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eServices such as occupational training and housing help.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eAftercare or long-term, follow-up services such as mutual-help groups or sober living environments to help them avoid relapse.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003c\\/ul\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eRecovery is a lifelong process\\u2014as is your support and encouragement along the way.\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003e\\n\"}\u003c/script\u003e\u003c/div\u003e\u003cdiv class='portal_accordion' id='portal_accordion-69a7511965f6f'\u003e\u003cscript crossorigin type='application/json'\u003e{\"props\":{\"title\":\"Co-Workers: What to Expect\"},\"children\":\"\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003ch2\u003eCo-Workers: What to Expect\u003c\\/h2\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eHandling suspected substance misuse with a colleague is very different than attempting to help a family member or friend. First of all, you should objectively document all incidents where you witness behaviors that suggest the individual may be misusing drugs or alcohol.\u003csup\u003e4 \u003c\\/sup\u003eReview your company\\u2019s human resources policy to ensure that you bring your documentation to the proper individual\\u2014your supervisor or someone in HR, perhaps\\u2014who can then determine the proper course of action to get your colleague the help they need.\u003csup\u003e5\u003c\\/sup\u003e\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003ch3\u003eThe Admissions Process\u003c\\/h3\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eWhile you may not be part of the admissions process, you can support your co-worker by being there for them and letting them know that you\\u2019re keeping their situation completely confidential. In addition, you can remind them about some of the benefits that may be available to them through work\\u2014such as an Employee Assistance Program (EAP), which can help employees with personal issues, including drug- and alcohol-related problems. You may also encourage them to look into the Family Medical Leave Act (\u003ca href=\\\"https:\\/\\/webapps.dol.gov\\/elaws\\/whd\\/fmla\\/10c9.aspx\\\" target=\\\"_blank\\\" rel=\\\"noopener\\\"\u003eFMLA\u003c\\/a\u003e), which allows eligible employees to take up to 12 weeks of unpaid leave to receive treatment for a substance use disorder.\u003csup\u003e6 \u003c\\/sup\u003e\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003ch3\u003eBefore Treatment\u003c\\/h3\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eYour colleague might be nervous, anxious, and scared about entering treatment. Some of the things that you can do to offer support include:\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cul\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eOffer help. Offer to assist with the practical tasks, such as childcare, house cleaning, pet sitting, and providing rides when necessary.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eBe there for them. Be supportive and encouraging.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003c\\/ul\u003e\\n\u003ch3\u003eAfter Treatment\u003c\\/h3\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eIt can be hard to know the proper way to behave around a colleague who returns from treatment for a substance use disorder. You should welcome your colleague back but respect their privacy. Don\\u2019t pry. Instead, let them decide if and when they want to bring up rehab.\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003ch3\u003eWhen and Why Seek Help\u003c\\/h3\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eAddiction is a treatable disease, and it can happen to anyone. In fact, 1 in 7 Americans report experiencing a substance use disorder, the clinical term for addiction.\u003csup\u003e1 \u003c\\/sup\u003e\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eWhile only a healthcare professional can diagnose a substance use disorder utilizing the criteria outlined in the \u003cem\u003eDiagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders \u003c\\/em\u003e(DSM-5), it may be helpful for you to be aware of the criteria used so you can spot the signs of a potential problem. The criteria clinicians use includes:\u003csup\u003e2\u003c\\/sup\u003e\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cul\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eTaking more of a substance than intended, or for longer periods than intended.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eHaving a persistent desire to cut back or stop using a substance but being unable to do so.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eSpending an increasing amount of time obtaining, using, or recovering from substance use.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eExperiencing cravings or intense urges to use a certain substance or substances.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eFailing to fulfill responsibilities at home, school, or work due to substance use.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eContinuing to use the substance despite having persistent social or relational problems that are caused by or worsened by use.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eGiving up previously important or meaningful activities or commitments due to substance use.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eRepeatedly using the substance in physically dangerous situations.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eContinuing to use the substance despite knowing that it causes or worsens ongoing physical or psychological problems.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eDeveloping a tolerance to the substance, which means they require more of the drug to achieve the desired effect or the effect is greatly reduced when using the same dose.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eExperiencing withdrawal symptoms when drastically cutting back or trying to abruptly stop substance use.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003c\\/ul\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eIf your co-worker experiences 2 or more of these symptoms in a 12-month period, they meet the diagnostic criteria for a substance use disorder.\u003csup\u003e2\u003c\\/sup\u003e\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eYou can help them get the treatment they need that is specific to them, their substance use history, and overall needs and treatment goals. A comprehensive treatment plan may include:\u003csup\u003e3\u003c\\/sup\u003e\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cul\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eBehavioral interventions to help them change their thoughts, emotions, and behaviors surrounding their substance use.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eContinued assessment so treatment can be modified to meet their changing needs.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eA variety of services to address their physical, mental, and emotional health.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eCoping skills training to help them identify triggers, manage stressors, and prevent future relapse.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eSubstance use education.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eServices such as occupational training and housing help.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eAftercare or long-term, follow-up services such as mutual-help groups or sober living environments to help them avoid relapse.\u003c\\/li\u003e\\n\u003c\\/ul\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eRecovery is a lifelong process\\u2014as is your support and encouragement along the way.\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003e\\n\"}\u003c/script\u003e\u003c/div\u003e\u003cdiv class='portal_accordion' id='portal_accordion-69a7511965fa3'\u003e\u003cscript crossorigin type='application/json'\u003e{\"props\":{\"title\":\"Veterans: What to Expect\"},\"children\":\"\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eFor information about Veteran admissions, \u003ca href=\\\"https:\\/\\/americanaddictioncenters.org\\/admissions\\/veterans\\\" target=\\\"_blank\\\" rel=\\\"noopener\\\"\u003eclick here\u003c\\/a\u003e.\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003e\\n\"}\u003c/script\u003e\u003c/div\u003e\u003cdiv class='portal_accordion' id='portal_accordion-69a7511966064'\u003e\u003cscript crossorigin type='application/json'\u003e{\"props\":{\"title\":\"Sources\"},\"children\":\"\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003col\u003e\\n\u003cli\u003eCenters for Disease Control and Prevention. type='application/json'\u003e{\"props\":{\"title\":\"FAQs\"},\"children\":\"\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003ch3\u003eDoes my insurance cover treatment?\u003c\\/h3\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eAAC works with most \u003ca href=\\\"https:\\/\\/americanaddictioncenters.org\\/insurance-providers\\\" target=\\\"_blank\\\" rel=\\\"noopener\\\"\u003einsurance providers\u003c\\/a\u003e. Our admissions navigators will assist you in verifying your specific insurance policy. We are able to guide you through what your coverage looks like at no cost, and the entire process is completely confidential.\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003ch3\u003eHow long will treatment last?\u003c\\/h3\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eDepending on the level of treatment and care needed, rehab can last anywhere from 5 to 90 days and beyond. Typically, detox can last between 5 and 7 days, while inpatient and residential programs can last between 30 and 90 days and beyond. AAC focuses on individualized treatment. We offer a full continuum of care with \u003ca href=\\\"https:\\/\\/americanaddictioncenters.org\\/drug-detox\\\" target=\\\"_blank\\\" rel=\\\"noopener\\\"\u003edetox\u003c\\/a\u003e, \u003ca href=\\\"https:\\/\\/americanaddictioncenters.org\\/rehab-guide\\/residential\\\" target=\\\"_blank\\\" rel=\\\"noopener\\\"\u003eresidential\u003c\\/a\u003e programs, all levels of \u003ca href=\\\"https:\\/\\/americanaddictioncenters.org\\/rehab-guide\\/outpatient-treatment\\\" target=\\\"_blank\\\" rel=\\\"noopener\\\"\u003eoutpatient\u003c\\/a\u003e care, and \u003ca href=\\\"https:\\/\\/americanaddictioncenters.org\\/telehealth\\\" target=\\\"_blank\\\" rel=\\\"noopener\\\"\u003etelehealth\u003c\\/a\u003e. You will be offered all levels of care and treated based on your specific needs. Our \u003ca href=\\\"https:\\/\\/americanaddictioncenters.org\\/admissions\\/navigator-team\\\" target=\\\"_blank\\\" rel=\\\"noopener\\\"\u003eadmissions navigators\u003c\\/a\u003e will assist you in determining what your specific treatment will look like at no cost and no obligation to enter treatment at one of our treatment facilities.\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003ch3\u003eWill I be uncomfortable during detox?\u003c\\/h3\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eAAC\u0026#8217;s detox is supervised by nurses and doctors 24\\/7 to ensure that you will be medically stable and as comfortable as possible. Our medical team individualizes your detox plan with the use of medications. Our main priority is your safety and comfort as you begin your journey to recovery.\u003c\\/p\u003e\\n\u003ch3\u003eHow quickly can I get admitted into treatment?\u003c\\/h3\u003e\\n\u003cp\u003eAt AAC, you may be able to enter a program immediately in order to medically detox and then move on to a formal treatment program to teach you the coping skills to get you on the road to long-term recovery. If you or a loved one struggle with a substance use disorder or an alcohol use disorder, please connect with one of our \u003ca Rehab Admissions Process

 
 
 
 Medical Detox 
 Insurance Accepted 
 Same Day Admission 

The moment you contact American Addiction Centers is the moment you can change your life. Recovery is possible. Don't wait. Take the first step today. Our team is here for you every step of the way.

 

Jump to Section:

 
 
 
 
 

Admissions Process

 
 

Intake Form

 
 
 
 

What to Expect

 
 
 

Who Answers?

 
 

Payment Options

 
 

Get Started

 
 
 

Additional Resources

What are the steps for getting into treatment?
 
If you are unsure where to begin with addiction treatment, we can help. We've outlined the steps you can expect to undergo during the admissions process for American Addiction Centers' (AAC) treatment facilities. However, if you find that an AAC facility is not the right fit for you, your admissions navigator will provide you with additional resources to help you find treatment elsewhere.

 
 
 

 

 

 
 

Online Intake Process

 

What to Expect
 
The admissions process can seem daunting at first, but we'll be with you every step of the way to answer any questions and make you feel as comfortable as possible.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Who Answers?
 
When you call our helpline an admissions navigator is available 24 /7 to help you or your loved one find treatment. They will listen to your story and you will have an opportunity to be truly heard. They will also help guide you through the admissions process and answer any questions you have about the steps to treatment.

 

Call now to get started on your recovery journey or learn more about our dedicated admissions team .

Payment Options
 

American Addiction Centers (AAC) customizes treatment for each individual, considering their specific needs. Your personalized plan will determine the exact costs. We believe that everyone requiring assistance should have access to addiction treatment, which is why we offer various payment options that can potentially be combined to help you cover the expenses.

 

 

Get Started
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Check to see if your insurance is in-network at an American Addiction Centers location.

 Step 1 /4 Check Coverage

Talking to your insurance plan administrator by calling the number on the back of your insurance card is a great place to start. The staff of addiction treatment facilities can also be of vital help, easing the path to payment so families have one less thing to worry over as they recover."}},{"@type":"Question","name:":"How to get financial assistance for substance abuse treatment?","acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Financial assistance for substance abuse treatment can come from federal, state, and local governments. There are also grants available through SAMHSA, as well as insurance options for certain populations, such as veterans or low-income families. Read more."}},{"@type":"Question","name:":"What is the cost of outpatient substance abuse treatment?","acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Outpatient treatment for substance abuse can cover many different services and levels of care. It is recommended that patients work with their insurance provider and the rehab facility directly to determine their out-of-pocket expenses. Read more."}},{"@type":"Question","name:":"Can I Get or Change My Health Insurance Coverage After a Qualifying Life Event?","acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Certain life changes, referred to as qualifying life events, allow you to make changes to existing coverage or sign up for a new health insurance plan. These life changes include loss of health coverage, changes in your household, changes in your residence, and more. Qualifying life events include:\r\n\r\n\r\nLosing existing job-based, individual, or student health coverage.\r\nLosing eligibility for Medicare, Medicaid, or Children’s Health Insurance Program (CHIP).\r\nTurning 26 years old and losing health coverage under your parent’s insurance plan.\r\nGetting married or divorced.\r\nHaving a baby or adopting a child.\r\nA death in your family that affects your health coverage.\r\nMoving to a different zip code or county that changes your health plan area.\r\nMoving as a student to or from the place where you will go to school.\r\nMoving to or from the place where you live and work as a seasonal worker.\r\nMoving to or from a shelter or other transitional housing.\r\nExperiencing changes in your income that affect your qualifying coverage.\r\nGaining membership in a federally recognized tribe or status as an Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act (ANCSA) Corporation shareholder.\r\nBecoming a U.S. citizen.\r\nLeaving incarceration such as jail or prison.\r\nStarting or ending service as an AmeriCorps member.\r\n\r\n\r\nAll situations are different, so these life events might require documentation—such as birth certificates, adoption records, marriage licenses, divorce papers, death certificates, rental agreements, or mortgages—to show that you have added family members, lost family members who provided the health coverage, or moved into a new health insurance plan area. These changes usually need to be done within 60 days of the life event, but check with you insurer on specifics."}},{"@type":"Question","name:":"Can I Have Multiple Insurance Providers?","acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Yes, you can have multiple insurance providers. When two (or more) health insurance plan providers cover the healthcare costs of one person, this is called coordination of benefits. You may have dual insurance coverage if:\r\n\r\n\r\nYou are married and covered under your insurance plan and your spouse’s.\r\nYou are under 26 years old and covered by your parents’ insurance and your own.\r\nYou are under 26 years old with divorced parents and are under both parents’ plan as a dependent.\r\n\r\nYou are over 65 years old and have coverage through your employer and Medicare.\r\nIf you have two separate health insurance plans, one plan will be your primary coverage and the other is your secondary coverage. Your primary provider pays first – up to its coverage limits. Your secondary insurance will then step in and pay the remaining cost (partial or full) of the treatment(s). Even after secondary insurance pays, you may still have some out-of-pocket costs."}}]}</script><meta name="next-head-count" content="31"/><meta charSet="UTF-8"/><meta http-equiv="X-UA-Compatible" content="IE=edge"/><meta name="theme-color" content="#1D2758"/><meta name="mobile-web-app-capable" content="yes"/><meta name="format-detection" content="telephone=no"/><meta name="google-site-verification" content="-kT5n2-IRln0edUW6ZUJ-R36qpRgybeZlzwo2f5Awns"/><link rel="icon" href="/static/favicon.ico" type="image/x-icon"/><link href="https://cdn.callrail.com" rel="preconnect" crossorigin="true"/><link href="https://www.googletagmanager.com" rel="preconnect" crossorigin="true"/><link rel="preload" as="font" href="/static/fonts/inter-regular-optimized.woff2" type="font/woff2" crossorigin="anonymous"/><link rel="preload" as="font" href="/static/fonts/inter-bold-optimized.woff2" type="font/woff2" crossorigin="anonymous"/><style>

class="jsx-123217fb97058cc4 jsx-3838467732 section__content"><div id="wordpress-content" class="jsx-123217fb97058cc4 jsx-3838467732"><div><h2>Does Your Health Insurance Cover Substance Use Treatment?</h2>
<p>Yes, health insurance plans will generally cover the cost of treatment for substance use disorder (drug and <a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/alcohol">alcohol</a> addiction) and mental health conditions. The particular health insurance plan that you have will determine how much of your treatment is covered by your insurance plan, as well as how much you will be required to pay out-of-pocket. It’s best to check with your insurance provider before trying to enter a treatment program for substance abuse issues to understand the details of what is covered under your plan.</p>
<p>Find out instantly if your insurance may be able to cover all or part of the cost of addiction treatment. Or call us today at <span class="portal_phone_link" id="portal_phone_link-69a753a81aed9"><script crossorigin type="application/json">{"props":{"helpline":"true"},"children":""}</script></span> You will speak with an admissions navigator, and they can help you through the verification process. American Addiction Centers can take the confusion out of contacting your insurance provider directly. Simply call us or fill in the form below and we can communicate with your insurance directly. By filling in our confidential form below, we can find out which treatment centers are in-network, we can advise you on the length of stay covered, and we can save you time and from the hassle of contacting your insurance company and/or looking through hard to understand insurance documents.</p>
<div class="portal_accordion" id="portal_accordion-69a753a81afaf"><script crossorigin type="application/json">{"props":{"title":"<strong>Health Insurance Glossary of Terms<\/strong>"},"children":"<\/p>\n<p>Before you continue reading, here are some terms and definitions that may help you better understand your insurance policy and substance abuse treatment coverage.<\/p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Deductible<\/strong>: The amount you pay for health care services before insurance coverage kicks in. If you have a $2,000 deductible, you pay the first $2,000, and then your insurance pays the rest. Sometimes you\u2019ll have a copayment or will need to pay for coinsurance for covered services after the deductible is met.<\/li>\n<li><strong>Copayment<\/strong>: The fixed amount you pay for health care services after the deductible is reached. For example, your copayment may be $20 per doctor visit.<\/li>\n<li><strong>Coinsurance<\/strong>: The percentage of costs you pay for health care services received after you\u2019ve met your deductible.<\/li>\n<li><strong>Out-of-Network<\/strong>: A health care provider or organization that does not have an agreement with your health insurance provider.<\/li>\n<li><strong>In-Network<\/strong> <strong>Provider<\/strong>. A healthcare provider or organization that has an agreement with your insurance provider to provide care at a discounted rate.<\/li>\n<li><strong>Out-of-pocket and out-of-pocket limit<\/strong>: Out-of-pocket expenses are those you will be financially responsible for paying on your own. The out-of-pocket limit is the most you will have to pay for services in a year.<\/li>\n<li><strong>Premium:<\/strong> The amount of money you must pay (monthly or annually) for your health insurance policy.<\/li>\n<\/ul>\n<p>\n"}</script></div>
<h2>Insurance Providers and Rehab Coverage</h2>
<p>Health insurance benefits are designed to make health care both affordable and accessible, and there’s no stigma attached to asking for help. People with addictions and insurance should use their coverage to the fullest in order to get the care they need to leave addictions behind for good. Talking to plan administrators is a great place to start, but remember that the staff of addiction treatment facilities can also be of vital help.</p>
<p>In some cases, they can smooth the path to payment, so families have one less thing to worry over as they recover. Explore more information about insurance providers and rehab coverage below. Call us at <span class="portal_phone_link" id="portal_phone_link-69a753a81afda"><script crossorigin type="application/json">{"props":{"helpline":"true"},"children":""}</script></span> If you do not see your provider listed below, we may still be able to work with you or your insurance provider to find treatment.</p>
<div class="portal_accordion-links" id="portal_accordion-links-insurance" data-title="" data-type="insurance"><script crossorigin type="application/json">{"props":{"type":"insurance"},"children":""}</script></div>
<p></p><div class="flex-grid-69a753a81b00c" style="">
		<br>
<div style="flex: 1; text-align: ;">
		
<h2><img loading="lazyel" decoding="async" class="alignnone wp-image-115447 size-full" alt="" width="500" height="334" sizes="(max-width: 500px) 100vw, 500px" data-src="https://admin.americanaddictioncenters.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/doctor-and-clipboard.jpg" src="data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20500%20334'%3E%3C/svg%3E" data-srcset="https://admin.americanaddictioncenters.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/doctor-and-clipboard.jpg 500w, https://admin.americanaddictioncenters.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/doctor-and-clipboard-300x200.jpg 300w"></h2>
<p>
	</p></div>
	
<div style="flex: 1; text-align: ;">
		
<h2>Options With and Without Insurance</h2>
<div class="portal_accordion" id="portal_accordion-69a753a81b1c9"><script crossorigin type="application/json">{"props":{"title":"What Types of Rehab Does Insurance Cover?"},"children":"<\/p>\n<p>There are various types of addiction treatment in the United States, most of which are often covered by health insurance policies. Nearly 90 percent of Americans have some form of health insurance in 2021.<sup>1<\/sup>\u00a0Depending on your insurance plan specifics, addiction treatment programs covered by insurance include:<\/p>\n<ul>\n<li><a title=\"Inpatient Care\" href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/rehab-guide\/inpatient\">Inpatient rehabilitation.<\/a><\/li>\n<li><a title=\"Outpatient Treatment\" href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/rehab-guide\/outpatient-treatment\">Outpatient care<\/a>.<\/li>\n<li><a href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/intensive-outpatient-programs\">Intensive outpatient.<\/a><\/li>\n<li><a title=\"Medical Detox\" href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/drug-detox\">Medical detoxification.<\/a><\/li>\n<li>Medication-assisted treatment.<\/li>\n<li><a title=\"Co-Occurring Mental Health Conditions\" href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/co-occurring-disorders\">Dual Diagnosis & Co-occurring mental health treatments<\/a>.<\/li>\n<li>Continuing care (e.g., counseling, therapy)<\/li>\n<li>Maintenance medication to support ongoing sobriety & other forms of\u00a0<a href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/addiction-medications\">MAT.<\/a><\/li>\n<\/ul>\n<p>\n"}</script></div>
<p></p><div class="portal_accordion" id="portal_accordion-69a753a81b205"><script crossorigin type="application/json">{"props":{"title":"Going to Rehab Without Insurance"},"children":"<p> If you don\u2019t have private health insurance, don\u2019t let this be a barrier to accessing treatment when you are ready. There are still various free rehab and state-funded rehab options available to you. States offer financial assistance for those who have no insurance and are in need free or low-cost addiction treatment. Google your state, county, or city to find low-cost substance use disorder programs and services. Many states offer free rehab or have state-funded rehab options, state insurance policies, and other ways to help those who need it.<\/p>\n<p>Other options for paying for drug and alcohol rehab include:<\/p>\n<p>Loans and\/or payment plans. Many treatment centers provide financing options that allow you to pay back the cost of treatment in small increments after treatment.<br \/>\nScholarships. Some treatment facilities offer scholarships to those in need to cover all or part of the cost. Some states also offer scholarships and grants to those with addiction who need help paying for treatment.<br \/>\nFinancial support from family. Though asking for help can be difficult, you may be surprised by how many people may want to support you. Ask family and friends for financial support in your endeavor to get sober. It\u2019s worth a try.<\/p>\n"}</script></div><br>
<div class="portal_accordion" id="portal_accordion-69a753a81b227"><script crossorigin type="application/json">{"props":{"title":"The Affordable Care Act (Obamacare) and Health Insurance Coverage"},"children":"<p> In March 2010, the Affordable Care Act was signed into law to make health insurance affordable and accessible to more people.2 The law also aimed to expand the Medicaid program and support innovative medical care delivery methods.<\/p>\n<p>The Affordable Care Act also states that no one can be denied access to health insurance, even if they have a preexisting condition like substance use disorder. The ACA lists substance use disorder as one of the 10 elements of essential health benefits that all healthcare insurers must provide. This means that all health insurance providers are required to provide coverage for mental health care and treatment.3 Both rehab for substance use disorder\/addiction and mental health care services fall under this mandate.<\/p>\n"}</script></div><br>

	</div>

<div style="flex: 1; text-align: ;">
		
<h2>Common Types of Healthcare Plans and Benefits</h2>
<p>The three most common healthcare plans are health maintenance organization (HMO) plans, preferred provider organization (PPO) plans, and point of sale (POS) plans.4 Substance abuse treatment and recovery may be covered by your insurance provider. Learn more about which plan, HMO or PPO, offers the best coverage:</p>
<div class="portal_accordion" id="portal_accordion-69a753a81b2ee"><script crossorigin type="application/json">{"props":{"title":"HMO (Health Maintenance Organization)"},"children":"<\/p>\n<p><a href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/insurance-coverage\/hmo\"><strong>HMO (Health Maintenance Organization)<\/strong><\/a>\u00a0plans allow patients to choose their primary care physician and see that doctor for most of their medical needs. This allows them to form a relationship with a doctor who knows their whole health history. When seeking a specialist or physician outside of the network with an HMO plan, a referral is needed by your primary care physician.<sup>5<\/sup>\u00a0HMOs have lower or no deductibles and overall coverage is usually a lower cost than PPO.<sup>6<\/sup>\u00a0Pros of HMO coverage are for those that are not seeking a specialist and healthcare providers out of their network and paying lower premiums.<sup>6\u00a0<\/sup>Health maintenance organizations cover healthcare costs for all in-network providers and services. You will be responsible for paying out-of-pocket if you seek care with a provider that is out-of-network with your insurance provider. HMOs generally offer lower monthly premiums and deductibles with set copays.<\/p>\n"}</script></div>
<div class="portal_accordion" id="portal_accordion-69a753a81b340"><script crossorigin type="application/json">{"props":{"title":"PPO (Preferred Provider Organization)"},"children":"<\/p>\n<p><a href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/insurance-coverage\/ppo\"><strong>PPO (Preferred Provider Organization)<\/strong><\/a>\u00a0plans allow patients to see healthcare providers in and out of their network without referrals.<sup>7<\/sup>\u00a0Preferred provider organization plans offer a little more flexibility in that they allow members to visit any doctor of their choosing, both in and out of network, but you\u2019ll pay less if you stay in-network. You don\u2019t need a primary care physician, and referrals are not required to see a specialist. PPO plans have higher monthly premiums and lower copays for treatments and services received. PPOs can have higher deductibles than those with an HMO plan.<sup>6<\/sup>\u00a0One of the pros of PPO coverage is having the option see specialists and other healthcare providers outside of your network without a referral from your primary care physician.<sup>6<\/sup><\/p>\n<p>\n"}</script></div>
<div class="portal_accordion" id="portal_accordion-69a753a81b362"><script crossorigin type="application/json">{"props":{"title":"POS (Point of Sale)"},"children":"<p><strong>POS (Point of Sale):<\/strong>\u00a0Point of sale plans are similar to HMO plans. The primary difference is that you can sometimes see care providers outside of your network in certain cases. This is what \u201cpoint of services\u201d means. Each insurance company varies with its rules on POS plans, so be sure to check the details of your specific insurer if this is the type of plan you have.<\/p>\n<p>\n"}</script></div><br>

	</div>
	
<p><img loading="lazyel" decoding="async" class="alignnone wp-image-64637 size-full" alt="" width="500" height="249" sizes="(max-width: 500px) 100vw, 500px" data-src="https://admin.americanaddictioncenters.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/12/hmo-ppo-chart-1-e1724970556772.png" src="data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20500%20249'%3E%3C/svg%3E" data-srcset="https://admin.americanaddictioncenters.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/12/hmo-ppo-chart-1-e1724970556772.png 500w, https://admin.americanaddictioncenters.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/12/hmo-ppo-chart-1-e1724970556772-300x149.png 300w"><br>

	</p></div>

<div class="flex-grid-69a753a81b384" style="">
		
<p></p><div style="flex: 1; text-align: ;">
		<br>
<img loading="lazyel" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-115446" alt="" width="500" height="359" sizes="(max-width: 500px) 100vw, 500px" data-src="https://admin.americanaddictioncenters.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/health-insurance.jpg" src="data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20500%20359'%3E%3C/svg%3E" data-srcset="https://admin.americanaddictioncenters.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/health-insurance.jpg 500w, https://admin.americanaddictioncenters.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/health-insurance-300x215.jpg 300w"><br>

	</div>
	
<p></p><div style="flex: 1; text-align: ;">
		
<h2>Cost and Coverage</h2>
<p></p><div class="portal_accordion" id="portal_accordion-69a753a81b47d"><script crossorigin type="application/json">{"props":{"title":"Drug Addiction Coverage and Cost"},"children":"<\/p>\n<p>Many healthcare insurance providers may be able to cover all or part of the\u00a0<a href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/rehab-guide\/rehab-cost\">cost of alcohol or drug rehab<\/a>. The cost of rehab varies depending on the patient\u2019s level of treatment, length of stay, <a href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/insurance-coverage\/anthem-blue-cross-blue-shield-colorado\">insurance coverage<\/a>, and other unique factors. The amount of coverage or insurance acceptance is based on the insurance policy that each patient is covered by. This means out-of-pocket expenses will vary.<\/p>\n<p>HealthCare.gov reports that plans participating in the insurance marketplace must provide care in 10 essential health categories, one of those being addiction care. Many private health insurance plans follow these same rules, too, so they could be sold on the marketplace at a later point in time. Most insurance policies don\u2019t separate drugs into \u201ccovered\u201d and \u201cnon-covered\u201d categories. If addiction treatments are considered a covered benefit, then care is provided to anyone who has an addiction, regardless of what that addiction is caused by. This is the same model health insurance programs use in order to treat other medical conditions.<\/p>\n<p>For example, some people develop obesity through overeating, while others develop weight difficulties due to hormonal or gland abnormalities. Health insurance programs that provide a weight-loss benefit don\u2019t cover one type of weight loss while eliminating the other. Doing so would make the plans much more expensive, as underwriters would need to delve deep into the medical history of each person needing care, and the plans would need to cover sophisticated tests in order to determine how the obesity came about. By just covering all obesity treatment, the plan can save money.<\/p>\n<p>The same is true for addictions. Plan administrators don\u2019t want to cover in-depth testing and interviews about what drugs were used, how the drugs were developed, where they came from, and what they\u2019re mixed with. Plans can keep things simple by covering all drugs, if they provide an addiction care benefit.<\/p>\n<p>Covering addiction care can also help states to keep costs down. For example, a PBS report suggests that incarcerating an adult for one year can cost up to $37,000, while providing residential care for addiction costs just $14,600. State-run plans might very well provide robust addiction care for all drugs simply because doing so could keep other costs in line.<br \/>\nPrivate plans might see benefits if addicted people don\u2019t land in expensive emergency rooms due to addictions or\u00a0<a href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/overdose\">overdoses<\/a>. By providing care for addictions, they might also reduce the number of organ transplants they might need to cover. When it comes to savings, robust care for all drugs might be the best way to go, and many insurance plans do just that.<\/p>\n<p>That means anyone with questions about what drugs are and aren\u2019t covered by insurance can just look at this page and get clear answers in just minutes. That page is a good first stop for anyone with addiction treatment questions.<\/p>\n<p>\n"}</script></div>
<p></p><div class="portal_accordion" id="portal_accordion-69a753a81b4b4"><script crossorigin type="application/json">{"props":{"title":"What Mental Health Issues Are Covered by Insurance?"},"children":"<\/p>\n<p>The legislation passed as part of the Affordable Care Act didn\u2019t stop with addiction care. Plans were also required, as part of the legislation, to provide the same level of care for mental health concerns that they do for physical health concerns. That means plans that provide doctor visits for a foot problem for $20 must also provide doctor visits for depression for $20. The care, and the cost, must be the same. The\u00a0<a title=\"American Psychological Association\" href=\"https:\/\/www.apa.org\/helpcenter\/parity-guide\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener noreferrer\">American Psychological Association<\/a>\u00a0says these parity laws apply to all sorts of programs, including those provided by employers, those coming through health care exchanges, and those coming through Medicaid and CHIP.<\/p>\n<p>Parity laws don\u2019t explicitly state what sorts of mental health conditions plans must provide care for, but the rules are similar to those seen in drug addiction. If plans provide care for mental illness, they typically don\u2019t specify that some illnesses are covered while others are not. That would require a huge amount of paperwork and time, and most plan administrators don\u2019t have much of either of those things to spare, so the plans simply cover all of it. Again, this is an issue that\u2019s best discussed with a plan administrator. But in general, fears that mental health issues won\u2019t be covered because they\u2019re \u201cbad\u201d are typically groundless. Health insurance just doesn\u2019t work that way.<\/p>\n"}</script></div>
<p></p><div class="portal_accordion" id="portal_accordion-69a753a81b52d"><script crossorigin type="application/json">{"props":{"title":"Are Treatment Medications Covered?"},"children":"<\/p>\n<p>Maintenance programs, part of\u00a0<a title=\"Aftercare Treatment\" href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/rehab-guide\/aftercare\">aftercare treatment<\/a>, are designed for people who can\u2019t get to a normal level of functioning in the absence of drugs, even if they\u2019ve been through a rehab program at a treatment center. Chemical alterations caused by drugs are just too severe in these people, and they need medications in order to correct those imbalances so they can live a life that\u2019s free of the influence of drugs.<\/p>\n<p>Maintenance treatment helps many people in recovery from certain substance use disorders, often long after the initial rehabilitation stay. For instance, opioid maintenance therapy on an opioid agonist medication like methadone or buprenorphine can help prevent withdrawal, minimize cravings and, over the longer-term, help to discourage relapse and allow that person to focus on sobriety and other day-to-day obligations.<\/p>\n<p>Maintenance medications like Suboxone, buprenorphine, and Antabuse are made for people with these sorts of issues, but they can be expensive. Thankfully, most experts suggest that insurance plans typically do cover these drugs.<\/p>\n<p>The National Alliance of Advocates for Buprenorphine Treatment says, for example, that most health insurance plans cover maintenance drugs for people in recovery from addictions to heroin and other similar drugs. The organization doesn\u2019t specify how many plans offer this coverage or what typical copayments might be, but the group seems confident that most plans do provide this benefit.<\/p>\n<p>The\u00a0<a title=\"Substance Abuse and Mental Health Service Administration\" href=\"https:\/\/www.samhsa.gov\/medication-assisted-treatment\/become-buprenorphine-waivered-practitioner\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener noreferrer\">Substance Abuse and Mental Health Service Administration<\/a>\u00a0says, on the other hand, that\u00a0<a href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/rehab-guide\/medicare\">Medicare<\/a>\u00a0and Medicaid plans will only cover these medications if their use is deemed vital for the ongoing health of the person in recovery. If the person is deemed somehow capable of healing without the medications, coverage wouldn\u2019t be provided under these plans.<br \/>\nThese two opposing views make it clear that plans can handle addiction maintenance care very differently. That\u2019s why it pays for families in need of this care to ask about coverage, copayments, and therapies before they start to get them. Some programs may have extensive coverage while others do not. <\/p>\n"}</script></div>
<p></p><div class="portal_accordion" id="portal_accordion-69a753a81b56f"><script crossorigin type="application/json">{"props":{"title":"The Cost of Rehab vs. The Cost of Addiction"},"children":"<\/p>\n<p>Addiction comes at a great cost: to the individual, family, friends, the community, and society at large. There is a great cost related to lost productivity, premature death, and crime.<sup>8<\/sup>\u00a0In fact, the societal cost of addiction is more than $532 billion a year.<sup>9<\/sup>\u00a0But more important than money is that treatment saves lives. If you or your loved one are ready to take the brave step to get treatment, the time is now.<\/p>\n<p>Whether you have full insurance coverage for drug and\u00a0<a href=\"https:\/\/americanaddictioncenters.org\/alcohol\/rehab-treatment\">alcohol addiction treatment<\/a>\u00a0or no insurance at all, getting treatment is worth it. With the right treatment plan, you can begin your path to recovery and long-term sobriety.<\/p>\n<p>\n"}</script></div><br>

	</div>

class="jsx-123217fb97058cc4 jsx-3838467732">Frequently Asked Questions</h2><div class="sc-260ded0-1 IUNLP"><div class="jsx-9ea4e4803dcef447 faq "><div class="jsx-9ea4e4803dcef447 faq__head"><div class="sc-5cf48062-0 jWSHgZ"><h3 class="faq__question">How to bill insurance for substance abuse treatment?</h3></div></div><div class="jsx-9ea4e4803dcef447 faq__body"><div class="jsx-9ea4e4803dcef447 faq__wrapper">People with addictions and insurance should use their coverage to the fullest in order to get the care they need. Talking to your insurance plan administrator by calling the number on the back of your insurance card is a great place to start. The staff of addiction treatment facilities can also be of vital help, easing the path to payment so families have one less thing to worry over as they recover.</div></div></div><div class="jsx-9ea4e4803dcef447 faq "><div class="jsx-9ea4e4803dcef447 faq__head"><div class="sc-5cf48062-0 jWSHgZ"><h3 class="faq__question">How to get financial assistance for substance abuse treatment?</h3></div></div><div class="jsx-9ea4e4803dcef447 faq__body"><div class="jsx-9ea4e4803dcef447 faq__wrapper">Financial assistance for substance abuse treatment can come from federal, state, and local governments. There are also grants available through SAMHSA, as well as insurance options for certain populations, such as veterans or low-income families. <a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/rehab-guide/public-assistance">Read more</a>.</div></div></div><div class="jsx-9ea4e4803dcef447 faq "><div class="jsx-9ea4e4803dcef447 faq__head"><div class="sc-5cf48062-0 jWSHgZ"><h3 class="faq__question">What is the cost of outpatient substance abuse treatment?</h3></div></div><div class="jsx-9ea4e4803dcef447 faq__body"><div class="jsx-9ea4e4803dcef447 faq__wrapper">Outpatient treatment for substance abuse can cover many different services and levels of care. It is recommended that patients work with their insurance provider and the rehab facility directly to determine their out-of-pocket expenses. <a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/intensive-outpatient-programs/cost">Read more</a>.</div></div></div><div class="jsx-9ea4e4803dcef447 faq "><div class="jsx-9ea4e4803dcef447 faq__head"><div class="sc-5cf48062-0 jWSHgZ"><h3 class="faq__question">Can I Get or Change My Health Insurance Coverage After a Qualifying Life Event?</h3></div></div><div class="jsx-9ea4e4803dcef447 faq__body"><div class="jsx-9ea4e4803dcef447 faq__wrapper">Certain life changes, referred to as qualifying life events, allow you to make changes to existing coverage or sign up for a new health insurance plan. These life changes include loss of health coverage, changes in your household, changes in your residence, and more. Qualifying life events include:

<ul>
<li>Losing existing job-based, individual, or student health coverage.</li>
<li>Losing eligibility for Medicare, Medicaid, or Children’s Health Insurance Program (CHIP).</li>
<li>Turning 26 years old and losing health coverage under your parent’s insurance plan.
Getting married or divorced.</li>
<li>Having a baby or adopting a child.</li>
<li>A death in your family that affects your health coverage.</li>
<li>Moving to a different zip code or county that changes your health plan area.</li>
<li>Moving as a student to or from the place where you will go to school.</li>
<li>Moving to or from the place where you live and work as a seasonal worker.</li>
<li>Moving to or from a shelter or other transitional housing.</li>
<li>Experiencing changes in your income that affect your qualifying coverage.</li>
<li>Gaining membership in a federally recognized tribe or status as an Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act (ANCSA) Corporation shareholder.</li>
<li>Becoming a U.S. citizen.</li>
<li>Leaving incarceration such as jail or prison.</li>
<li>Starting or ending service as an AmeriCorps member.</li>
</ul>

All situations are different, so these life events might require documentation—such as birth certificates, adoption records, marriage licenses, divorce papers, death certificates, rental agreements, or mortgages—to show that you have added family members, lost family members who provided the health coverage, or moved into a new health insurance plan area. These changes usually need to be done within 60 days of the life event, but check with you insurer on specifics.</div></div></div><div class="jsx-9ea4e4803dcef447 faq "><div class="jsx-9ea4e4803dcef447 faq__head"><div class="sc-5cf48062-0 jWSHgZ"><h3 class="faq__question">Can I Have Multiple Insurance Providers?</h3></div></div><div class="jsx-9ea4e4803dcef447 faq__body"><div class="jsx-9ea4e4803dcef447 faq__wrapper">Yes, you can have multiple insurance providers. When two (or more) health insurance plan providers cover the healthcare costs of one person, this is called coordination of benefits. You may have dual insurance coverage if:

<ul>
<li>You are married and covered under your insurance plan and your spouse’s.</li>
<li>You are under 26 years old and covered by your parents’ insurance and your own.</li>
<li>You are under 26 years old with divorced parents and are under both parents’ plan as a dependent.</li>

You are over 65 years old and have coverage through your employer and Medicare.
If you have two separate health insurance plans, one plan will be your primary coverage and the other is your secondary coverage. Your primary provider pays first – up to its coverage limits. Your secondary insurance will then step in and pay the remaining cost (partial or full) of the treatment(s). Even after secondary insurance pays, you may still have some out-of-pocket costs.</div></div></div></div></section><section class="jsx-123217fb97058cc4 jsx-3838467732 section__next-steps"><div
